title: Turkic peoples

**Turkic peoples** are a collection of diverse [ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group") of [West](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia"), [Central](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), [East](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia"), and [North Asia](/wiki/North_Asia "North Asia") as well as parts of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe"), who speak [Turkic languages](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages").[[35]](#cite_note-Britannica_Definition-36)[[36]](#cite_note-Yunusbayev_Definition-37)

According to historians and linguists, the [Proto-Turkic language](/wiki/Proto-Turkic_language "Proto-Turkic language") originated in Central-East Asia,[[37]](#cite_note-38) potentially in the [Altai-Sayan region](/wiki/Altai-Sayan_region "Altai-Sayan region"), [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia") or [Tuva](/wiki/Tuva "Tuva").[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGolden201137–38-39)[[39]](#cite_note-Uchiyama_2020-40)[[40]](#cite_note-Lee_2017-41) Initially, Proto-Turkic speakers were potentially both hunter-gatherers and farmers; they later became [nomadic](/wiki/Nomadic "Nomadic") [pastoralists](/wiki/Pastoralists "Pastoralists").[[41]](#cite_note-42) Early and [medieval](/wiki/Post-classical_history "Post-classical history") Turkic groups exhibited a wide range of both East Asian and West-Eurasian physical appearances and genetic origins, in part through long-term contact with neighboring peoples such as [Iranic](/wiki/Iranic "Iranic"), [Mongolic](/wiki/Mongolic_peoples "Mongolic peoples"), [Tocharian](/wiki/Tocharians "Tocharians"), [Uralic](/wiki/Uralic_people "Uralic people") and [Yeniseian](/wiki/Yeniseian_people "Yeniseian people") peoples.[[42]](#cite_note-43)

Many vastly differing [ethnic groups](/wiki/Ethnic_group "Ethnic group") have throughout history become part of the Turkic peoples through [language shift](/wiki/Language_shift "Language shift"), [acculturation](/wiki/Acculturation "Acculturation"), [conquest](/wiki/Conquest "Conquest"), [intermixing](/wiki/Interracial_marriage "Interracial marriage"), [adoption](/wiki/Adoption "Adoption"), and [religious conversion](/wiki/Religious_conversion "Religious conversion").[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYunusbayev_et_al.2015-1) Nevertheless, Turkic peoples share, to varying degrees, non-linguistic characteristics like cultural traits, ancestry from a common [gene pool](/wiki/Gene_pool "Gene pool"), and historical experiences.[[1]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYunusbayev_et_al.2015-1) Some of the most notable modern Turkic ethnic groups include the [Altai people](/wiki/Altai_people "Altai people"), [Azerbaijanis](/wiki/Azerbaijanis "Azerbaijanis"), [Chuvash people](/wiki/Chuvash_people "Chuvash people"), [Gagauz people](/wiki/Gagauz_people "Gagauz people"), [Kazakhs](/wiki/Kazakhs "Kazakhs"), [Kyrgyz people](/wiki/Kyrgyz_people "Kyrgyz people"), [Turkmens](/wiki/Turkmens "Turkmens"), [Turkish people](/wiki/Turkish_people "Turkish people"), [Tuvans](/wiki/Tuvans "Tuvans"), [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs"), [Uzbeks](/wiki/Uzbeks "Uzbeks"), and [Yakuts](/wiki/Yakuts "Yakuts").

## Etymology

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/D%C4%ABw%C4%81n_Lugh%C4%81t_al-Turk_%28original%29.jpg/250px-D%C4%ABw%C4%81n_Lugh%C4%81t_al-Turk_%28original%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:D%C4%ABw%C4%81n_Lugh%C4%81t_al-Turk_(original).jpg)

Map from [Kashgari](/wiki/Mahmud_al-Kashgari "Mahmud al-Kashgari")'s *[Diwan](/wiki/D%C4%ABw%C4%81n_Lugh%C4%81t_al-Turk "Dīwān Lughāt al-Turk")* (11th century), showing the distribution of Turkic tribes.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bf/Turkic_Head_of_Koltegin_Statue_%2835324303410%29.jpg/250px-Turkic_Head_of_Koltegin_Statue_%2835324303410%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turkic_Head_of_Koltegin_Statue_(35324303410).jpg)

[Bust of Kul Tigin](/wiki/Bust_of_Kul_Tigin "Bust of Kul Tigin") (AD 684–731), prince of the [Second Turkic Khaganate](/wiki/Second_Turkic_Khaganate "Second Turkic Khaganate"), found in [Khashaat](/wiki/Khashaat "Khashaat"), [Arkhangai Province](/wiki/Arkhangai_Province "Arkhangai Province"), [Orkhon River](/wiki/Orkhon_River "Orkhon River") valley. [National Museum of Mongolia](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Mongolia "National Museum of Mongolia").

The first known mention of the term *Turk* ([Old Turkic](/wiki/Old_Turkic "Old Turkic"): 𐱅𐰇𐰼𐰰 *Türük* or 𐱅𐰇𐰼𐰰:𐰜𐰇𐰛 *Kök Türük*, [Chinese](/wiki/Chinese_language "Chinese language"): 突厥; [pinyin](/wiki/Pinyin "Pinyin"): *Tūjué* < [Middle Chinese](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese") \**tɦut-kyat* < \**dwət-kuɑt*, [Old Tibetan](/wiki/Old_Tibetan "Old Tibetan"): *drugu*)[[43]](#cite_note-KulteginMC-44)[[44]](#cite_note-TonyukukMC-45)[[45]](#cite_note-46)[[46]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGolden2011''Ethnogenesis_in_the_tribal_zone:_The_Shaping_of_the_Turks''-47) applied to only one Turkic group, namely, the [Göktürks](/wiki/G%C3%B6kt%C3%BCrks "Göktürks"),[[47]](#cite_note-48) who were also mentioned, as *türüg* ~ *török*, in the 6th-century [Khüis Tolgoi inscription](/wiki/Inscription_of_H%C3%BCis_Tolgoi "Inscription of Hüis Tolgoi"), most likely not later than 587 AD.[[48]](#cite_note-49)[[49]](#cite_note-50)[[50]](#cite_note-51) A letter by [Ishbara Qaghan](/wiki/Ishbara_Qaghan "Ishbara Qaghan") to [Emperor Wen of Sui](/wiki/Emperor_Wen_of_Sui "Emperor Wen of Sui") in 585 described him as "the Great Turk Khan".[[51]](#cite_note-52)[[52]](#cite_note-53) The [Bugut](/wiki/Bugut_inscription "Bugut inscription") (584 CE) and [Orkhon inscriptions](/wiki/Orkhon_inscriptions "Orkhon inscriptions") (735 CE) use the terms *Türküt*, *Türk* and *Türük*.[[53]](#cite_note-54)

During the first century CE, [Pomponius Mela](/wiki/Pomponius_Mela "Pomponius Mela") refers to the *Turcae* in the forests north of the [Sea of Azov](/wiki/Sea_of_Azov "Sea of Azov"), and [Pliny the Elder](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder") lists the *Tyrcae* among the people of the same area.[[54]](#cite_note-55)[[55]](#cite_note-56)[[56]](#cite_note-57) However, English archaeologist [Ellis Minns](/wiki/Ellis_Minns "Ellis Minns") contended that *Tyrcae* Τῦρκαι is "a false correction" for *[Iyrcae](/wiki/Iyrcae "Iyrcae")* Ἱύρκαι, a people who dwelt beyond the [Thyssagetae](/wiki/Thyssagetae "Thyssagetae"), according to [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") ([Histories](/wiki/Histories_(Herodotus) "Histories (Herodotus)"), iv. 22), and were likely [Ugric](/wiki/Ugric_peoples "Ugric peoples") ancestors of [Magyars](/wiki/Magyars "Magyars").[[57]](#cite_note-58) There are references to certain groups in antiquity whose names might have been foreign transcriptions of *Tür(ü)k*, such as [*Togarma*](/wiki/Togarmah#Turkic_history "Togarmah"), *Turukha*/*Turuška*, [*Turukku*](/wiki/Turukkaeans "Turukkaeans") and so on; but the information gap is so substantial that any connection of these ancient people to the modern Turks is not possible.[[58]](#cite_note-59)[[59]](#cite_note-60)

The Chinese *[Book of Zhou](/wiki/Book_of_Zhou "Book of Zhou")* (7th century) presents an etymology of the name *Turk* as derived from 'helmet', explaining that this name comes from the shape of a mountain where they worked in the [Altai Mountains](/wiki/Altai_Mountains "Altai Mountains").[[60]](#cite_note-61) Hungarian scholar [András Róna-Tas](/wiki/Andr%C3%A1s_R%C3%B3na-Tas "András Róna-Tas") (1991) pointed to a Khotanese-Saka word, *tturakä* 'lid', semantically stretchable to 'helmet', as a possible source for this folk etymology, yet Golden thinks this connection requires more data.[[61]](#cite_note-Golden2006-62)

It is generally accepted that the name *Türk* is ultimately derived from the [Old-Turkic](/wiki/Old_Turkic_language "Old Turkic language") migration-term[[62]](#cite_note-63) 𐱅𐰇𐰼𐰰 *Türük*/*Törük*,[[63]](#cite_note-Nisanyan-64) which means 'created, born'[[64]](#cite_note-65) or 'strong'.[[65]](#cite_note-66) Turkologist Peter B. Golden agrees that the term *Turk* has roots in [Old Turkic](/wiki/Old_Turkic "Old Turkic"),[[66]](#cite_note-67) yet is not convinced by attempts to link *Dili*, *Dingling*, *Chile*, *Tele*, and *Tiele*, which possibly transcribed \**tegrek* (probably meaning '[cart](/wiki/Cart "Cart")'), to *Tujue*, which transliterated to *Türküt*.[[67]](#cite_note-68)

Scholars, including Toru Haneda, Onogawa Hidemi, and Geng Shimin believed that *Di*, *Dili*, *Dingling*, *Chile* and *Tujue* all came from the Turkic word *Türk*, which means 'powerful' and 'strength', and its plural form is *Türküt*.[[68]](#cite_note-69) Even though [Gerhard Doerfer](/wiki/Gerhard_Doerfer "Gerhard Doerfer") supports the proposal that *türk* means 'strong' in general, [Gerard Clauson](/wiki/Gerard_Clauson "Gerard Clauson") points out that "the word *türk* is never used in the generalized sense of 'strong'" and that *türk* was originally a noun and meant "'the culminating point of maturity' (of a fruit, human being, etc.), but more often used as an [adjective] meaning (of a fruit) 'just fully ripe'; (of a human being) 'in the prime of life, young, and vigorous'".[[69]](#cite_note-70) Hakan Aydemir (2022) also contends that *Türk* originally did not mean "strong, powerful" but "gathered; united, allied, confederated" and was derived from the Pre-[Proto-Turkic](/wiki/Proto-Turkic "Proto-Turkic") verb \**türü* "heap up, collect, gather, assemble".[[70]](#cite_note-71)

The earliest Turkic-speaking peoples identifiable in Chinese sources are the [Yenisei Kyrgyz](/wiki/Yenisei_Kyrgyz "Yenisei Kyrgyz") and [Xinli](/wiki/Xueyantuo "Xueyantuo"), located in South Siberia.[[71]](#cite_note-72)[[72]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGolden201127-73)[[note 2]](#cite_note-78) Another example of an early Turkic population would be the [Dingling](/wiki/Dingling "Dingling").[[77]](#cite_note-79)[[78]](#cite_note-80)[[79]](#cite_note-81)

In Late Antiquity itself, as well as in and the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), the name "Scythians" was used in [Greco-Roman](/wiki/Greco-Roman "Greco-Roman") and [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine_literature "Byzantine literature") literature for various groups of nomadic "[barbarians](/wiki/Barbarian "Barbarian")" living on the Pontic-Caspian Steppe who were not related to the actual Scythians.[[80]](#cite_note-Application-82)[[81]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEIvantchik2018-83) Medieval European chroniclers subsumed various Turkic peoples of the Eurasian steppe as "Scythians". Between 400 CE and the 16th century, Byzantine sources use the name Σκύθαι (*Skuthai*) in reference to twelve different Turkic peoples.[[82]](#cite_note-G._Moravcsik,_p._236-39-84)

In the modern Turkish language as used in the Republic of Turkey, a distinction is made between "Turks" and the "Turkic peoples" in loosely speaking: the term *Türk* corresponds specifically to the "Turkish-speaking" people (in this context, "Turkish-speaking" is considered the same as "Turkic-speaking"), while the term *[Türki](/wiki/Turki "Turki")* refers generally to the people of modern "Turkic Republics" (*Türki Cumhuriyetler* or *Türk Cumhuriyetleri*). However, the proper usage of the term is based on [the linguistic classification](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages") in order to avoid any [political](/wiki/Political "Political") sense. In short, the term *Türki* can be used for *Türk* or vice versa.[[83]](#cite_note-85)

3. **[^](#cite_ref-86)** [Book of Wei](/wiki/Book_of_Wei "Book of Wei") [vol. 102.](https://zh.wikisource.org/wiki/%E9%AD%8F%E6%9B%B8/%E5%8D%B7102#%E7%83%8F%E5%AD%AB-%E7%96%8F%E5%8B%92-%E6%82%85%E8%88%AC) quote: "悅般國 [...] 其風俗言語與高車同" translation: "Yueban nation [...] Their customs and language are the same as the Gaoche['s]"; Gaoche (高車; lit. "High-Carts") was another name of the Turkic-speaking Tiele
4. **[^](#cite_ref-89)** Merkits were always counted as a part of the Mongols within the Mongol Empire, however, some scholars proposed additional Turkic ancestry for Merkits; Christopher P. Atwood – *Encyclopedia of Mongolia and the Mongol Empire* [ISBN](/wiki/ISBN_(identifier) "ISBN (identifier)") [978-0-8160-4671-3](/wiki/Special:BookSources/978-0-8160-4671-3 "Special:BookSources/978-0-8160-4671-3"), Facts on File, Inc. 2004.
5. **[^](#cite_ref-90)** Refers to forest peoples of the North, including the Turkic-speaking [Tuvans](/wiki/Tuvans "Tuvans") and [Yakuts](/wiki/Yakuts "Yakuts"), and also Mongolic-speaking [Altai Uriankhai](/wiki/Altai_Uriankhai "Altai Uriankhai"). The ethnonym Uriankhai is etymologically Mongolic, compare [Khalkha](/wiki/Khalkha_Mongolian "Khalkha Mongolian") *uria(n)* "war motto" and *khai*, [alternation](/wiki/Alternation_(linguistics) "Alternation (linguistics)") of *[khan](/wiki/Khan_(title) "Khan (title)")*. Uriankhai people are possibly linked to the Wuluohun tribe of the [Shiwei people](/wiki/Shiwei_people "Shiwei people"), who were predominantly Mongolic-speaking.
6. **[^](#cite_ref-98)** Even though Chinese historians routinely ascribed Xiongnu origin to various nomadic peoples, such ascriptions do not necessarily indicate the subjects' exact origins; for examples, Xiongnu ancestry was ascribed to Turkic-speaking Göktürks and Tiele as well as Para-Mongolic-speaking Kumo Xi and Khitan.[[92]](#cite_note-97)

## Language

Main articles: [Turkic languages](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages") and [Proto-Turkic language](/wiki/Proto-Turkic_language "Proto-Turkic language")

Further information: [List of alphabets used by Turkic languages](/wiki/List_of_alphabets_used_by_Turkic_languages "List of alphabets used by Turkic languages")

### Distribution

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Carte_peuples_turcs.png/330px-Carte_peuples_turcs.png)](/wiki/File:Carte_peuples_turcs.png)

Descriptive map of Turkic peoples.

The Turkic languages constitute a [language family](/wiki/Language_family "Language family") of some 30 languages, spoken across a vast area from [Eastern Europe](/wiki/Eastern_Europe "Eastern Europe") and the [Mediterranean](/wiki/Mediterranean "Mediterranean"), to [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") and [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria") and through to the Middle East. Some 170 million people have a Turkic language as their native language;[[93]](#cite_note-distribution-99) an additional 20 million people speak a Turkic language as a [second language](/wiki/Second_language "Second language"). The Turkic language with the greatest number of speakers is [Turkish proper](/wiki/Turkish_language "Turkish language"), or [Anatolian](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia") Turkish, the speakers of which account for about 40% of all Turkic speakers.[[94]](#cite_note-LanguagesOfTheWorld-100) More than one third of these are ethnic [Turks of Turkey](/wiki/Turks_of_Turkey "Turks of Turkey"), dwelling predominantly in Turkey proper and formerly [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire")-dominated areas of Southern and Eastern Europe and [West Asia](/wiki/West_Asia "West Asia"); as well as in Western Europe, Australia and the Americas as a result of immigration. The remainder of the Turkic people are concentrated in Central Asia, Russia, the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"), China, and northern Iraq.

The Turkic language family was traditionally considered to be part of the proposed [Altaic language family](/wiki/Altaic_language_family "Altaic language family").[[95]](#cite_note-101) Howeover since the 1950s, a majority of linguists have rejected the proposal, after supposed [cognates](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") were found not to be valid, hypothesized sound shifts were not found, and Turkic and [Mongolic languages](/wiki/Mongolic_languages "Mongolic languages") were found to be converging rather than diverging over the centuries. Opponents of the theory proposed that the similarities are due to [mutual linguistic influences](/wiki/Language_contact "Language contact") between the groups concerned.[[96]](#cite_note-102)[[97]](#cite_note-103)[[98]](#cite_note-104)[[99]](#cite_note-perel2012-105)[[100]](#cite_note-106)

### Alphabet

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9f/Codex_Cumanicus_58.jpg/250px-Codex_Cumanicus_58.jpg)](/wiki/File:Codex_Cumanicus_58.jpg)

A page from "[Codex Kumanicus](/wiki/Codex_Cumanicus "Codex Cumanicus")". The [Codex](/wiki/Codex "Codex") was designed in order to help [Catholic](/wiki/Catholic "Catholic") [missionaries](/wiki/Missionaries "Missionaries") communicate with the [Kumans](/wiki/Kumans "Kumans").

The Turkic alphabets are sets of related alphabets with letters (formerly known as [runes](/wiki/Runes "Runes")), used for writing mostly [Turkic languages](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages"). Inscriptions in Turkic alphabets were found in [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia"). Most of the preserved inscriptions were dated to between 8th and 10th centuries CE.

The earliest positively dated and read Turkic inscriptions date from the 8th century, and the alphabets were generally replaced by the [Old Uyghur alphabet](/wiki/Old_Uyghur_alphabet "Old Uyghur alphabet") in the [East](/wiki/East_Asia "East Asia") and [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia"), Arabic script in the Middle and Western Asia, [Cyrillic](/wiki/Cyrillic "Cyrillic") in Eastern Europe and in the Balkans, and [Latin alphabet](/wiki/Latin_alphabet "Latin alphabet") in Central Europe. The latest recorded use of [Turkic alphabet](/wiki/Turkic_alphabets_(disambiguation) "Turkic alphabets (disambiguation)") was recorded in Central Europe's Hungary in 1699 CE.

The Turkic [runiform](/wiki/Old_Turkic_alphabet "Old Turkic alphabet") scripts, unlike other typologically close scripts of the world, do not have a uniform [palaeography](/wiki/Palaeography "Palaeography") as do, for example, the [Gothic](/wiki/Gothic_language "Gothic language") runiform scripts, noted for their exceptional uniformity of language and paleography.[[101]](#cite_note-107) The Turkic alphabets are divided into four groups, the best known of which is the [Orkhon](/wiki/Orkhon_script "Orkhon script") version of the Enisei group. The Orkhon script is the alphabet used by the [Göktürks](/wiki/G%C3%B6kt%C3%BCrk "Göktürk") from the 8th century to record the [Old Turkic language](/wiki/Old_Turkic_language "Old Turkic language"). It was later used by the [Uyghur Empire](/wiki/Uyghur_Empire "Uyghur Empire"); a [Yenisei](/wiki/Yenisei "Yenisei") variant is known from 9th-century [Kyrgyz](/wiki/Kyrgyz_people "Kyrgyz people") inscriptions, and it has likely cousins in the [Talas Valley](/wiki/Talas_Valley "Talas Valley") of [Turkestan](/wiki/Turkestan "Turkestan") and the [Old Hungarian script](/wiki/Old_Hungarian_script "Old Hungarian script") of the 10th century. [Irk Bitig](/wiki/Irk_Bitig "Irk Bitig") is the only known complete manuscript text written in the Old Turkic script.[[102]](#cite_note-tekin-1993-1-108)

### Origins

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Modern_Turkic_People_PCA_Analysis.png/500px-Modern_Turkic_People_PCA_Analysis.png)](/wiki/File:Modern_Turkic_People_PCA_Analysis.png)

Ancestral composition of modern Turkic‐speaking populations based on supervised admixture, with modeled proportions of [Ancient Northeast Asian](/wiki/Ancient_Northeast_Asian "Ancient Northeast Asian") ancestry (Mongolia\_N\_North and [Ashina](/wiki/Empress_Ashina "Empress Ashina"),  ), as well as Chinese Central Plain millet farmers (YR\_LN,  ) and West Eurasian‐related ancestry ([Russia\_Sintashta\_MLBA](/wiki/Sintashta "Sintashta"),  ).[[103]](#cite_note-109)

The origins of the Turkic peoples has been a topic of much discussion.[[104]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEYunusbayev_et_al.20151–2-110)[[105]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-111) [Peter Benjamin Golden](/wiki/Peter_Benjamin_Golden "Peter Benjamin Golden") proposes two locations for the Proto-Turkic Urheimat: the southern [Altai-Sayan](/wiki/Altai-Sayan "Altai-Sayan") region,[[38]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEGolden201137–38-39) and Southern [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"), from [Lake Baikal](/wiki/Lake_Baikal "Lake Baikal") to eastern [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia").[[106]](#cite_note-112) Other studies suggested an early presence of Turkic peoples in Mongolia,[[107]](#cite_note-113)[[39]](#cite_note-Uchiyama_2020-40) or [Tuva](/wiki/Tuva "Tuva").[[40]](#cite_note-Lee_2017-41)

A possible genealogical link of the Turkic languages to Mongolic and Tungusic languages, specifically a hypothetical homeland in [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria"), such as proposed in the [Transeurasian hypothesis](/wiki/Altaic_languages "Altaic languages"), by [Martine Robbeets](/wiki/Martine_Robbeets "Martine Robbeets"), has received support but also criticism, with opponents attributing similarities to long-term contact.[[108]](#cite_note-114)[[109]](#cite_note-115)[[110]](#cite_note-116) The proto-Turkic-speakers may be linked to [Neolithic](/wiki/Neolithic "Neolithic") [East Asian](/wiki/East_Asian_people "East Asian people") agricultural societies in [Northeastern China](/wiki/Northeastern_China "Northeastern China"), which is to be associated with the [Xinglongwa culture](/wiki/Xinglongwa_culture "Xinglongwa culture") and the succeeding [Hongshan culture](/wiki/Hongshan_culture "Hongshan culture"), based on varying degrees of specific East Asian genetic substratum among modern Turkic speakers.[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson_et_al.2020-117)[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi_et_al.2020-118)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUchiyamaGillamSavelyevNing2020-119) According to historians, "the Proto-Turkic subsistence strategy included an agricultural component, a tradition that ultimately went back to the origin of millet agriculture in Northeast China".[[111]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTENelson_et_al.2020-117)[[112]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELi_et_al.2020-118)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUchiyamaGillamSavelyevNing2020-119) This view is however questioned by other geneticists, who found no evidence for a shared "Neolithic Hongshan ancestry", but in contrary primary [Ancient Northeast Asian](/wiki/Ancient_Northeast_Asian "Ancient Northeast Asian") (ANA) Neolithic ancestry from the [Amur region](/wiki/Amur "Amur"), supporting an origin from Northeast Asia rather than Manchuria.[[114]](#cite_note-He_230–250-120)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Turkic_origin_and_expansion.png/250px-Turkic_origin_and_expansion.png)](/wiki/File:Turkic_origin_and_expansion.png)

According to Uchiyama et al. 2020 the "ultimate Proto-Turkic homeland may have been located in a more compact area, most likely in Eastern Mongolia".[[115]](#cite_note-121)

Around 2,200 BC, the (agricultural) ancestors of the Turkic peoples probably migrated westwards into [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia"), where they adopted a pastoral lifestyle, in part borrowed from [Iranian peoples](/wiki/Iranian_peoples "Iranian peoples"). Given nomadic peoples such as [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu"), [Rouran](/wiki/Rouran "Rouran") and [Xianbei](/wiki/Xianbei "Xianbei") share underlying genetic ancestry "that falls into or close to the northeast Asian gene pool", the proto-Turkic language likely originated in northeastern Asia.[[116]](#cite_note-122)

Genetic data found that almost all modern Turkic peoples retained at least some shared ancestry associated with populations in "South Siberia and Mongolia" (SSM), supporting this region as the "Inner Asian Homeland (IAH) of the pioneer carriers of Turkic languages" which subsequently expanded into Central Asia. The main [Turkic expansion](/wiki/Turkic_expansion "Turkic expansion") took place during the 5th–16th centuries, partially overlapping with the [Mongol Empire](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire") period. Based on single-path IBD tracts, the common Turkic ancestral population lived prior to these migration events, and likely stem from a similar source population as [Mongolic peoples](/wiki/Mongolic_peoples "Mongolic peoples") further East. Historical data suggests that the Mongol Empire period acted as secondary force of "turkification", as the Mongol conquest "did not involve massive re-settlements of Mongols over the conquered territories. Instead, the Mongol war machine was progressively augmented by various Turkic tribes as they expanded, and in this way Turkic peoples eventually reinforced their expansion over the Eurasian steppe and beyond."[[105]](#cite_note-ReferenceA-111)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b0/Population_structure_of_Turkic-speaking_populations_in_the_context_of_their_geographic_neighbors_across_Eurasia.PNG/250px-Population_structure_of_Turkic-speaking_populations_in_the_context_of_their_geographic_neighbors_across_Eurasia.PNG)](/wiki/File:Population_structure_of_Turkic-speaking_populations_in_the_context_of_their_geographic_neighbors_across_Eurasia.PNG)

Population structure of Turkic-speaking populations in the context of their geographic neighbors across Eurasia. Turkic-speaking populations are shown in red. The upper barplot shows only Turkic-speaking populations.

There is increasing evidence for a partial continuity from the [eastern Scythians](/wiki/Scytho-Siberian_world "Scytho-Siberian world") to the Turkic-speakers of the Altai region.[[117]](#cite_note-123) Turkic-speaking Central Asians can be described as having formed from mixture between [Scythian](/wiki/Scytho-Siberian_world "Scytho-Siberian world")-like groups, displaying their highest genetic affinity to modern day [Tajiks](/wiki/Tajiks "Tajiks"), and "Eastern Steppe Xiongnu" groups during the [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age").[[118]](#cite_note-Dai_2022-124)

A 2018 [autosomal](/wiki/Autosomal "Autosomal") [single-nucleotide polymorphism](/wiki/Single-nucleotide_polymorphism "Single-nucleotide polymorphism") study suggested that the [Eurasian Steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_Steppe "Eurasian Steppe") slowly transitioned from [Indo European](/wiki/Indo_European "Indo European") and [Iranian](/wiki/Iranian_languages "Iranian languages")-speaking groups with largely western Eurasian ancestry to increasing East Asian ancestry with Turkic and Mongolian groups in the past 4000 years, including extensive Turkic migrations out of Mongolia and slow assimilation of local populations.[[119]](#cite_note-125)[[113]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEUchiyamaGillamSavelyevNing2020-119) A 2022 report suggested that Turkic and Mongolic populations in Central Asia formed via admixture events during the Iron Age between "local [Indo-Iranian](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") and a South-Siberian or Mongolian group with a high East-Asian ancestry (around 60%)". Modern day [Turkmens](/wiki/Turkmens "Turkmens") form an outlier among Central Asian Turkic-speakers with a lower frequency of the Baikal component (c. 22%) and a lack of the Han-like component, being closer to other Indo-Iranian groups.[[120]](#cite_note-126) A subsequent study in 2022 also found that the spread of Turkic-speaking populations into Central Asia happened after the spread of Indo-European speakers into the area.[[118]](#cite_note-Dai_2022-124) Another 2022 study found that all Altaic‐speaking (Turkic, Tungusic, and Mongolic) populations "were a mixture of dominant Siberian Neolithic ancestry and non-negligible YRB ancestry", suggesting their origins were somewhere in Northeast Asia, most likely the [Amur river basin](/wiki/Amur_river "Amur river"). Except Eastern and Southern Mongolic-speakers, all "possessed a high proportion of West Eurasian-related ancestry, in accordance with the linguistically documented language borrowing in Turkic languages".[[114]](#cite_note-He_230–250-120)

A 2023 study analyzed the DNA of [Empress Ashina](/wiki/Empress_Ashina "Empress Ashina") (568–578 AD), a Royal Göktürk, whose remains were recovered from a mausoleum in [Xianyang](/wiki/Xianyang "Xianyang"), [China](/wiki/China "China").[[121]](#cite_note-Yang_2023-127) The authors determined that Empress Ashina belonged to the North-East Asian [mtDNA](/wiki/MtDNA "MtDNA") haplogroup [F1d](/wiki/Haplogroup_F_(mtDNA) "Haplogroup F (mtDNA)"), and that approximately 96-98% of her autosomal ancestry was of [Ancient Northeast Asian](/wiki/Ancient_Northeast_Asian "Ancient Northeast Asian") origin, while roughly 2-4% was of West Eurasian origin, indicating ancient admixture.[[121]](#cite_note-Yang_2023-127) This study weakened the "western Eurasian origin and multiple origin hypotheses".[[121]](#cite_note-Yang_2023-127) However, they also noted that "Central Steppe and early Medieval Türk exhibited a high but variable degree of West Eurasian ancestry, indicating there was a genetic substructure of the Türkic empire."[[121]](#cite_note-Yang_2023-127) The early medieval Türk samples were modelled as having 37.8% West Eurasian ancestry and 62.2% Ancient Northeast Asian ancestry[[122]](#cite_note-128) and historic Central Steppe Türk samples were also an admixture of West Eurasian and Ancient Northeast Asian ancestry,[[123]](#cite_note-129) while historic Karakhanid, Kipchak and the Turkic Karluk samples had 50.6%-61.1% West Eurasian ancestry and 38.9%–49.4% Iron Age [Yellow River](/wiki/Yellow_River "Yellow River") farmer ancestry.[[124]](#cite_note-130) A 2020 study also found "high genetic heterogeneity and diversity during the Türkic and Uyghur periods" in the early medieval period in [Eastern Eurasian Steppe](/wiki/Eurasian_Steppe#Mongolian-Manchurian_Steppe_(Eastern_Steppe) "Eurasian Steppe").[[125]](#cite_note-Jeong_et_al_2020-131)

The earliest separate Turkic peoples, such as the *Gekun* (鬲昆) and *Xinli* (薪犁), appeared on the peripheries of the late [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu") confederation about 200 BCE[[126]](#cite_note-Sima_Qian_110-132)[[127]](#cite_note-Peter_Zieme_2006,_p._64-133) (contemporaneous with the Chinese [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty"))[[128]](#cite_note-Findley_2005,_p._29-134) and later among the Turkic-speaking [Tiele](/wiki/Tiele_people "Tiele people")[[129]](#cite_note-135) as *[Hegu](/wiki/Yenisei_Kyrgyz "Yenisei Kyrgyz")* (紇骨)[[130]](#cite_note-Pulleyblank_1990-136) and *[Xue](/wiki/Xueyantuo "Xueyantuo")* (薛).[[73]](#cite_note-Pulleyblank_p._VII-74)[[74]](#cite_note-Duan_p._370-75)

The [Tiele](/wiki/Tiele_people "Tiele people") (also known as Gaoche 高車, lit. "High Carts"),[[131]](#cite_note-137) may be related to the [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu") and the [Dingling](/wiki/Dingling "Dingling").[[132]](#cite_note-138) According to the *[Book of Wei](/wiki/Book_of_Wei "Book of Wei")*, the Tiele people were the remnants of the Chidi (赤狄), the red [Di](/wiki/Beidi "Beidi") people competing with the [Jin](/wiki/Jin_(Chinese_state) "Jin (Chinese state)") in the [Spring and Autumn period](/wiki/Spring_and_Autumn_period "Spring and Autumn period").[[133]](#cite_note-139) Historically they were established after the 6th century BCE.[[127]](#cite_note-Peter_Zieme_2006,_p._64-133)

The Tiele were first mentioned in Chinese literature from the 6th to 8th centuries.[[134]](#cite_note-Cheng2012-140) Some scholars (Haneda, Onogawa, Geng, etc.) proposed that *Tiele*, *Dili*, *Dingling*, *Chile*, *Tele*, & *Tujue* all transliterated underlying *Türk*; however, [Golden](/wiki/Peter_Benjamin_Golden "Peter Benjamin Golden") proposed that *Dili*, *Dingling*, *Chile*, *Tele*, and *Tiele* transliterated *Tegrek* while Tujue transliterated *Türküt*, plural of *Türk*.[[135]](#cite_note-141) The appellation *Türük* ([Old Turkic](/wiki/Old_Turkic "Old Turkic"): 𐱅𐰇𐰼𐰰) ~ *Türk* (OT: 𐱅𐰇𐰼𐰚) (whence [Middle Chinese](/wiki/Middle_Chinese "Middle Chinese") 突厥 \**dwət-kuɑt* > \**tɦut-kyat* > [standard Chinese](/wiki/Standard_Chinese "Standard Chinese"): *Tūjué*) was initially reserved exclusively for the [Göktürks](/wiki/G%C3%B6kt%C3%BCrks "Göktürks") by Chinese, Tibetans, and even the Turkic-speaking [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghur_Khaganate "Uyghur Khaganate"). In contrast, medieval Muslim writers, including Turkic speakers like Ottoman historian [Mustafa Âlî](/wiki/Mustafa_%C3%82l%C3%AE "Mustafa Âlî") and explorer [Evliya Çelebi](/wiki/Evliya_%C3%87elebi "Evliya Çelebi") as well as [Timurid](/wiki/Timurid_Empire "Timurid Empire") scientist [Ulugh Beg](/wiki/Ulugh_Beg "Ulugh Beg"), often viewed Inner Asian tribes, "as forming a single entity regardless of their linguistic affiliation" commonly used Turk as a generic name for Inner Asians (whether Turkic- or Mongolic-speaking). Only in modern era do modern historians use Turks to refer to all peoples speaking [Turkic languages](/wiki/Turkic_languages "Turkic languages"), differentiated from non-Turkic speakers.[[136]](#cite_note-142)

According to some researchers (Duan, Xue, Tang, Lung, Onogawa, etc.) the later [Ashina tribe](/wiki/Ashina_tribe "Ashina tribe") descended from the [Tiele confederation](/wiki/Tiele_people "Tiele people").[[137]](#cite_note-LT-143)[[138]](#cite_note-144)[[139]](#cite_note-145)[[140]](#cite_note-146)[[141]](#cite_note-147) The Tiele however were probably one of many early Turkic groups, ancestral to later Turkic populations.[[142]](#cite_note-148)[[143]](#cite_note-149) However, according to Lee & Kuang (2017), Chinese histories do not describe the Ashina and the Göktürks as descending from the Dingling or the Tiele confederation.[[144]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017201-202-150)

#### Xiongnu (3rd c. BCE – 1st c. CE)

Main article: [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu")

See also: [Huns](/wiki/Huns "Huns")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/XiongnuMap.png/250px-XiongnuMap.png)](/wiki/File:XiongnuMap.png)

Territory of the Xiongnu, which included [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia"), Western [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria"), [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang"), East [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"), East [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan "Kyrgyzstan"), [Inner Mongolia](/wiki/Inner_Mongolia "Inner Mongolia"), and [Gansu](/wiki/Gansu "Gansu").

It has even been suggested that the Xiongnu themselves, who were mentioned in Han dynasty records, were [Proto-Turkic](/wiki/Proto-Turkic "Proto-Turkic") speakers.[[145]](#cite_note-151)[[146]](#cite_note-152)[[147]](#cite_note-153)[[148]](#cite_note-154) The Turks may ultimately have been of Xiongnu descent.[[149]](#cite_note-155) Although little is known for certain about the Xiongnu language(s), it seems likely that at least a considerable part of Xiongnu tribes spoke a Turkic language.[[150]](#cite_note-156) Some scholars believe they were probably a confederation of various ethnic and linguistic groups.[[151]](#cite_note-157)[[152]](#cite_note-158) According to a study by Alexander Savelyev and Choongwon Jeong, published in 2020 in the journal *Evolutionary Human Sciences* by Cambridge University Press, "the predominant part of the Xiongnu population is likely to have spoken Turkic". However, genetic studies found a mixture of western and eastern Eurasian ancestries, suggesting a large genetic diversity within the Xiongnu. The Turkic-related component may be brought by eastern Eurasian genetic substratum.[[153]](#cite_note-159)

Using the only extant possibly Xiongnu writings, the rock art of the [Yinshan](/wiki/Yinshan "Yinshan") and [Helan Mountains](/wiki/Helan_Mountains "Helan Mountains"),[[154]](#cite_note-160) some scholars argue that the older Xiongnu writings are precursors to the earliest known [Turkic](/wiki/Old_Turkic "Old Turkic") alphabet, the [Orkhon script](/wiki/Orkhon_script "Orkhon script"). Petroglyphs of this region dates from the [9th millennium BCE](/wiki/9th_millennium_BCE "9th millennium BCE") to the 19th century, and consists mainly of engraved signs (petroglyphs) and few painted images.[[155]](#cite_note-161) Excavations done during 1924–1925 in [Noin-Ula](/wiki/Noin-Ula "Noin-Ula") kurgans located in the [Selenga](/wiki/Selenga "Selenga") River in the northern [Mongolian](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia") hills north of [Ulaanbaatar](/wiki/Ulaanbaatar "Ulaanbaatar") produced objects with over 20 carved characters, which were either identical or very similar to the [runic](/wiki/Runic "Runic") letters of the Turkic Orkhon script discovered in the [Orkhon Valley](/wiki/Orkhon_Valley "Orkhon Valley").[[156]](#cite_note-162)

### Steppe expansions

#### Göktürks – Turkic Khaganate (5th–8th c.)
