The First Turkic Khaganate at its greatest extent, in 576, with neighbouring contemporary polities.[[157]](#cite_note-163)

Main articles: [Göktürks](/wiki/G%C3%B6kt%C3%BCrks "Göktürks") and [First Turkic Khaganate](/wiki/First_Turkic_Khaganate "First Turkic Khaganate")

The earliest certain mentioning of the politonym "Turk" was in the Chinese [Book of Zhou](/wiki/Book_of_Zhou "Book of Zhou"). In the 540s AD, this text mentions that the Turks came to China's border seeking silk goods and a trade relationship. A Sogdian diplomat represented China in a series of embassies between the [Western Wei](/wiki/Western_Wei "Western Wei") dynasty and the Turks in the years 545 and 546.[[158]](#cite_note-164)

According to the *Book of Sui* and the *[Tongdian](/wiki/Tongdian "Tongdian")*, they were "mixed barbarians" ([雜胡](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/%E9%9B%9C%E8%83%A1 "wikt:雜胡"); *záhú*) who migrated from [Pingliang](/wiki/Pingliang "Pingliang") (now in modern [Gansu province](/wiki/Gansu_province "Gansu province"), [China](/wiki/China "China")) to the [Rourans](/wiki/Rouran "Rouran") seeking inclusion in their confederacy and protection from the prevailing dynasty.[[159]](#cite_note-Suishu_84_tujue-165)[[160]](#cite_note-Tong197-166) Alternatively, according to the *[Book of Zhou](/wiki/Book_of_Zhou "Book of Zhou")*, *[History of the Northern Dynasties](/wiki/History_of_the_Northern_Dynasties "History of the Northern Dynasties")*, and *[New Book of Tang](/wiki/New_Book_of_Tang "New Book of Tang")*, the Ashina clan was a component of the [Xiongnu](/wiki/Xiongnu "Xiongnu") confederation.[[161]](#cite_note-Zhou50-167)[[162]](#cite_note-168)[[163]](#cite_note-169)[[164]](#cite_note-170) Göktürks were also posited as having originated from an obscure Suo state (索國), north of the Xiongnu.[[165]](#cite_note-171)[[166]](#cite_note-172) The Ashina tribe were famed [metalsmiths](/wiki/Metalsmith "Metalsmith") and were granted land south of the [Altai Mountains](/wiki/Altai_Mountains "Altai Mountains") (金山 *Jinshan*), which looked like a [helmet](/wiki/Helmet "Helmet"), from which they were said to have gotten their name 突厥 (*Tūjué*),[[167]](#cite_note-173)[[159]](#cite_note-Suishu_84_tujue-165) the first recorded use of "Turk" as a political name. In the 6th-century, Ashina's power had increased such that they conquered the Tiele on their Rouran overlords' behalf and even overthrew Rourans and established the First Turkic Khaganate.[[168]](#cite_note-Carter_V._Findley-174)

The original [Old Turkic](/wiki/Old_Turkic "Old Turkic") name *Kök Türk* derives from *kök* ~ *kö:k*, "sky, sky-coloured, blue, blue-grey".[[169]](#cite_note-175) Unlike its Xiongnu predecessor, the Göktürk Khaganate had its temporary [Khagans](/wiki/Khagan "Khagan") from the [Ashina](/wiki/Ashina_tribe "Ashina tribe") clan, who were *subordinate* to a [sovereign](/wiki/Sovereignty "Sovereignty") authority controlled by a council of tribal chiefs. The [Khaganate](/wiki/Khaganate "Khaganate") retained elements of its original [animistic-](/wiki/Animism "Animism") [shamanistic](/wiki/Shamanistic "Shamanistic") religion, that later evolved into [Tengriism](/wiki/Tengriism "Tengriism"), although it received missionaries of [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") monks and practiced a syncretic religion. The Göktürks were the first Turkic people to write [Old Turkic](/wiki/Old_Turkic "Old Turkic") in a runic script, the [Orkhon script](/wiki/Orkhon_script "Orkhon script"). The Khaganate was also the first state known as "Turk". It eventually collapsed due to a series of dynastic conflicts, but many states and peoples later used the name "Turk".[[170]](#cite_note-176)[[171]](#cite_note-177)

The Göktürks ([First Turkic Kaganate](/wiki/First_Turkic_Kaganate "First Turkic Kaganate")) quickly spread west to the Caspian Sea. Between 581 and 603 the [Western Turkic Khaganate](/wiki/Western_Turkic_Khaganate "Western Turkic Khaganate") in Kazakhstan separated from the [Eastern Turkic Khaganate](/wiki/Eastern_Turkic_Khaganate "Eastern Turkic Khaganate") in Mongolia and [Manchuria](/wiki/Manchuria "Manchuria") during a civil war. The Han-Chinese successfully overthrew the Eastern Turks in 630 and created a military Protectorate until 682. After that time the [Second Turkic Khaganate](/wiki/Second_Turkic_Khaganate "Second Turkic Khaganate") ruled large parts of the former Göktürk area. After several wars between Turks, Chinese and Tibetans, the weakened Second Turkic Khaganate was replaced by the [Uyghur Khaganate](/wiki/Uyghur_Khaganate "Uyghur Khaganate") in the year 744.[[172]](#cite_note-Haywood,_John_1998-178)

#### Bulgars, Golden Horde and the Siberian Khanate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/00/Bulgarians_and_Slavs_VI-VII_century.png/250px-Bulgarians_and_Slavs_VI-VII_century.png)](/wiki/File:Bulgarians_and_Slavs_VI-VII_century.png)

The migration of the Bulgars after the fall of [Old Great Bulgaria](/wiki/Old_Great_Bulgaria "Old Great Bulgaria") in the 7th century

The [Bulgars](/wiki/Bulgars "Bulgars") established themselves in between the Caspian and Black Seas in the 5th and 6th centuries, followed by their conquerors, the [Khazars](/wiki/Khazars "Khazars") who converted to Judaism in the 8th or 9th century. After them came the [Pechenegs](/wiki/Pechenegs "Pechenegs") who created a large confederacy, which was subsequently taken over by the [Cumans](/wiki/Cumans "Cumans") and the [Kipchaks](/wiki/Kipchaks "Kipchaks"). One group of Bulgars settled in the Volga region and mixed with local [Volga Finns](/wiki/Volga_Finns "Volga Finns") to become the [Volga Bulgars](/wiki/Volga_Bulgars "Volga Bulgars") in what is today [Tatarstan](/wiki/Tatarstan "Tatarstan"). These Bulgars were conquered by the Mongols following their westward sweep under [Ogedei Khan](/wiki/Ogedei_Khan "Ogedei Khan") in the 13th century.[[173]](#cite_note-179) Other Bulgars settled in Southeastern Europe in the 7th and 8th centuries, and mixed with the [Slavic](/wiki/Slavs "Slavs") population, adopting what eventually became the Slavic [Bulgarian language](/wiki/Bulgarian_language "Bulgarian language"). Everywhere, Turkic groups mixed with the local populations to varying degrees.[[168]](#cite_note-Carter_V._Findley-174)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1d/GoldenHorde1300.png/250px-GoldenHorde1300.png)](/wiki/File:GoldenHorde1300.png)

[Golden Horde](/wiki/Golden_Horde "Golden Horde")

The Volga Bulgaria became an Islamic state in 922 and influenced the region as it controlled many trade routes. In the 13th century, Mongols invaded Europe and established the [Golden Horde](/wiki/Golden_Horde "Golden Horde") in Eastern Europe, western and northern Central Asia, and even western Siberia. The [Cuman-Kipchak Confederation](/wiki/Cuman-Kipchak_Confederation "Cuman-Kipchak Confederation") and Islamic [Volga Bulgaria](/wiki/Volga_Bulgaria "Volga Bulgaria") were absorbed by the Golden Horde in the 13th century; in the 14th century, Islam became the official religion under [Uzbeg Khan](/wiki/Uzbeg_Khan "Uzbeg Khan") where the general population (Turks) as well as the aristocracy (Mongols) came to speak the [Kipchak language](/wiki/Kipchak_language "Kipchak language") and were collectively known as "[Tatars](/wiki/Tatars "Tatars")" by Russians and Westerners. This country was also known as the [Kipchak Khanate](/wiki/Kipchak_Khanate "Kipchak Khanate") and covered most of what is today [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine"), as well as the entirety of modern-day southern and eastern [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") (the European section). The Golden Horde disintegrated into several khanates and hordes in the 15th and 16th century including the [Crimean Khanate](/wiki/Crimean_Khanate "Crimean Khanate"), [Khanate of Kazan](/wiki/Khanate_of_Kazan "Khanate of Kazan"), and [Kazakh Khanate](/wiki/Kazakh_Khanate "Kazakh Khanate") (among others), which were one by one conquered and annexed by the Russian Empire in the 16th through 19th centuries.[[174]](#cite_note-180)

In Siberia, the [Siberian Khanate](/wiki/Siberian_Khanate "Siberian Khanate") was established in the 1490s by fleeing Tatar aristocrats of the disintegrating [Golden Horde](/wiki/Golden_Horde "Golden Horde") who established Islam as the official religion in western Siberia over the partly Islamized native [Siberian Tatars](/wiki/Siberian_Tatars "Siberian Tatars") and indigenous Uralic peoples. It was the northernmost Islamic state in recorded history and it survived up until 1598 when it was conquered by Russia.[[175]](#cite_note-181)

#### Uyghur Khaganate (8th–9th c.)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b5/Uyghur_Khaganate.png/250px-Uyghur_Khaganate.png)](/wiki/File:Uyghur_Khaganate.png)

Uyghur Khaganate

[![Uyghur painting from the Bezeklik cavels from the 9th century](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/aa/Ancient_Uyghur_Art.jpg/250px-Ancient_Uyghur_Art.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ancient_Uyghur_Art.jpg)

Uyghur painting from the Bezeklik murals

Main article: [Uyghur Khaganate](/wiki/Uyghur_Khaganate "Uyghur Khaganate")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/97/Museum_f%C3%BCr_Indische_Kunst_Dahlem_Berlin_Mai_2006_063.jpg/250px-Museum_f%C3%BCr_Indische_Kunst_Dahlem_Berlin_Mai_2006_063.jpg)](/wiki/File:Museum_f%C3%BCr_Indische_Kunst_Dahlem_Berlin_Mai_2006_063.jpg)

Uyghur royals in Chinese-style dresses

The [Uyghur Khaganate](/wiki/Uyghur_Khaganate "Uyghur Khaganate") had established itself by the year 744 AD.[[176]](#cite_note-182) Through trade relations established with China, its capital city of [Ordu Baliq](/wiki/Ordu_Baliq "Ordu Baliq") in central Mongolia's [Orkhon Valley](/wiki/Orkhon_Valley "Orkhon Valley") became a wealthy center of commerce,[[177]](#cite_note-183) and a significant portion of the Uyghur population abandoned their nomadic lifestyle for a [sedentary](/wiki/Sedentary "Sedentary") one. The Uyghur Khaganate produced extensive literature, and a relatively high number of its inhabitants were literate.[[178]](#cite_note-184)

The official state religion of the early Uyghur Khaganate was [Manichaeism](/wiki/Manichaeism "Manichaeism"), which was introduced through the conversion of [Bögü Qaghan](/wiki/B%C3%B6g%C3%BC_Qaghan "Bögü Qaghan") by the [Sogdians](/wiki/Sogdians "Sogdians") after the [An Lushan rebellion](/wiki/An_Lushan_rebellion "An Lushan rebellion").[[179]](#cite_note-185) The Uyghur Khaganate was tolerant of religious diversity and practiced variety of religions including Buddhism, Christianity, shamanism and Manichaeism.[[180]](#cite_note-186)

During the same time period, the [Shatuo](/wiki/Shatuo "Shatuo") Turks emerged as power factor in Northern and Central China and were recognized by the Tang Empire as allied power.

In 808, 30,000 Shatuo under Zhuye Jinzhong defected from the Tibetans to Tang China and the Tibetans punished them by killing Zhuye Jinzhong as they were chasing them.[[181]](#cite_note-187) The Uyghurs also fought against an alliance of Shatuo and Tibetans at Beshbalik.[[182]](#cite_note-188)

The Shatuo Turks under Zhuye Chixin ([Li Guochang](/wiki/Li_Guochang "Li Guochang")) served the Tang dynasty in fighting against their fellow Turkic people in the [Uyghur Khaganate](/wiki/Uyghur_Khaganate "Uyghur Khaganate"). In 839, when the Uyghur khaganate (Huigu) general Jueluowu (掘羅勿) rose against the rule of then-reigning [Zhangxin Khan](/wiki/Zhangxin_Khan "Zhangxin Khan"), he elicited the help from Zhuye Chixin by giving Zhuye 300 horses, and together, they defeated Zhangxin Khan, who then committed suicide, precipitating the subsequent collapse of the Uyghur Khaganate. In the next few years, when Uyghur Khaganate remnants tried to raid Tang borders, the Shatuo participated extensively in counterattacking the Uyghur Khaganate with other tribes loyal to Tang.[[183]](#cite_note-189) In 843, Zhuye Chixin, under the command of the Han Chinese officer [Shi Xiong](/wiki/Shi_Xiong "Shi Xiong") with Tuyuhun, Tangut and Han Chinese troops, participated in a raid against the Uyghur khaganate that led to the slaughter of Uyghur forces at Shahu mountain.[[184]](#cite_note-190)[[185]](#cite_note-191)[[186]](#cite_note-192)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Later_Tang.png/250px-Later_Tang.png)](/wiki/File:Later_Tang.png)

The Turkic Later Tang dynasty

The [Shatuo](/wiki/Shatuo "Shatuo") Turks had founded several short-lived [sinicized](/wiki/Sinicized "Sinicized") dynasties in northern China during the [Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period](/wiki/Five_Dynasties_and_Ten_Kingdoms_period "Five Dynasties and Ten Kingdoms period") starting with Later Tang. The Shatuo chief Zhuye Chixin's family was adopted by the Tang dynasty and given the title prince of Jin and the Tang dynasty imperial surname of Li, which is why the Shatuo of Later Tang claimed to be restoring the Tang dynasty and not founding a new one. The official language of these dynasties was Chinese and they used Chinese titles and names. Some Shaotuo Turk emperors (of the Later Jin, Later Han and Northern Han) also claimed patrilineal Han Chinese ancestry.[[187]](#cite_note-193)[[188]](#cite_note-vol._1-194)[[189]](#cite_note-ancestry-195)

After the fall of the Tang dynasty in 907, the Shatuo Turks replaced them and created the [Later Tang](/wiki/Later_Tang "Later Tang") dynasty in 923. The Shatuo Turks ruled over a large part of northern China, including [Beijing](/wiki/Beijing "Beijing"). They adopted Chinese names and united Turkic and Chinese traditions. Later Tang fell in 937 but the Shatuo rose to become a powerful faction of northern China. They created two other dynasties, including the [Later Jin](/wiki/Later_Jin_(Five_Dynasties) "Later Jin (Five Dynasties)") and [Later Han](/wiki/Later_Han_(Five_Dynasties) "Later Han (Five Dynasties)") and [Northern Han](/wiki/Northern_Han "Northern Han") (Later Han and Northern Han were ruled by the same family, with the latter being a rump state of the former). The Shatuo Liu Zhiyuan was a Buddhist and he worshipped the [Mengshan Giant Buddha](/wiki/Mengshan_Giant_Buddha "Mengshan Giant Buddha") in 945. The Shatuo dynasties were replaced by the Han Chinese [Song dynasty](/wiki/Song_dynasty "Song dynasty").[[190]](#cite_note-Theobald-196)[[191]](#cite_note-197) The Shatuo became the [Ongud](/wiki/Ongud "Ongud") Turks living in Inner Mongolia after the Song dynasty conquered the last Shatuo dynasty of Northern Han.[[192]](#cite_note-198)[[193]](#cite_note-Paulillo,_Mauricio_2013_pp._237-252-199) The Ongud assimilated to the Mongols.[[194]](#cite_note-Aristov-200)[[195]](#cite_note-Ochir-201)[[196]](#cite_note-202)[[193]](#cite_note-Paulillo,_Mauricio_2013_pp._237-252-199)

The Yenisei Kyrgyz allied with China to destroy the Uyghur Khaganate in the year 840 AD.[[172]](#cite_note-Haywood,_John_1998-178)[[190]](#cite_note-Theobald-196) From the [Yenisei River](/wiki/Yenisei_River "Yenisei River"), the Kyrgyz pushed south and eastward in to Xinjiang and the [Orkhon Valley](/wiki/Orkhon_Valley "Orkhon Valley") in central Mongolia, leaving much of the Uyghur civilization in ruins.[[197]](#cite_note-203) Much of the Uyghur population relocated to the southwest of Mongolia, establishing the [Ganzhou Uyghur Kingdom](/wiki/Ganzhou_Uyghur_Kingdom "Ganzhou Uyghur Kingdom") in Gansu where their descendants are the modern day [Yugurs](/wiki/Yugurs "Yugurs") and [Qocho Kingdom](/wiki/Qocho_Kingdom "Qocho Kingdom") in Turpan, Xinjiang.[[198]](#cite_note-204)

### Central Asia

#### Kangar union (659–750)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/AD_659KangarUnion.png/250px-AD_659KangarUnion.png)](/wiki/File:AD_659KangarUnion.png)

Kangar Union after the fall of Western Turkic Khaganate, 659–750

The Kangar Union (*Qanghar Odaghu*) was a [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_people "Turkic people") state in the former territory of the Western Turkic Khaganate (the entire present-day state of [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"), without [Zhetysu](/wiki/Zhetysu "Zhetysu")). The capital of the Kangar union was located in the Ulytau mountains. Among the Pechenegs, the **Kangar**[[note 1]](#cite_note-205) formed the elite of the Pecheneg tribes. After being defeated by the [Kipchaks](/wiki/Kipchaks "Kipchaks"), [Oghuz Turks](/wiki/Oghuz_Turks "Oghuz Turks"), and the [Khazars](/wiki/Khazars "Khazars"), they migrated west and defeated [Magyars](/wiki/Magyars "Magyars"),[[199]](#cite_note-206) and after forming an alliance with the [Bulgars](/wiki/Bulgars "Bulgars"), they defeated the [Byzantine](/wiki/Byzantine "Byzantine") Army.[[200]](#cite_note-207) The Pecheneg state was established by the 11th century and at its peak carried a population of over 2.5 million, composed of many different ethnic groups.[[201]](#cite_note-208)

The elite of the Kangar tribes are believed to have had an [Iranian](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians") origin,[[202]](#cite_note-209) and they likely spoke an Iranian language,[[203]](#cite_note-210) while most of the Pecheneg population spoke a Turkic language, with a significant percentage speaking [Hunno-Bulgar](/wiki/Hunno-Bulgar_languages "Hunno-Bulgar languages") dialects.

The Yatuks, a tribe within the Kangar state who could not accompany the Kangars as they migrated West, remained in the old lands, where they are known as the [Kangly](/wiki/Kangly "Kangly") people, who are now part of the [Uzbek](/wiki/Uzbeks "Uzbeks"), [Kazakh](/wiki/Kazakhs "Kazakhs"), and [Karakalpak](/wiki/Karakalpaks "Karakalpaks") tribes.[[204]](#cite_note-211)

#### Oghuz Yabgu State (766–1055)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/40/AD_750OguzYabgu.png/250px-AD_750OguzYabgu.png)](/wiki/File:AD_750OguzYabgu.png)

Oghuz Yabgu State (c.750 CE)

The Oguz Yabgu State (*Oguz il*, meaning "Oguz Land", "Oguz Country")(750–1055) was a [Turkic](/wiki/Turkic_people "Turkic people") state, founded by [Oghuz Turks](/wiki/Oghuz_Turks "Oghuz Turks") in 766, located geographically in an area between the coasts of the [Caspian](/wiki/Caspian_Sea "Caspian Sea") and [Aral Seas](/wiki/Aral_Sea "Aral Sea"). Oguz tribes occupied a vast territory in [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan") along the [Irgiz](/wiki/Irgiz_River_(Kazakhstan) "Irgiz River (Kazakhstan)"), [Yaik](/wiki/Yaik "Yaik"), [Emba](/wiki/Emba_River "Emba River"), and [Uil](/wiki/Uil "Uil") rivers, the Aral Sea area, the [Syr Darya](/wiki/Syr_Darya "Syr Darya") valley, the foothills of the [Karatau Mountains](/wiki/Karatau_Mountains "Karatau Mountains") in [Tien-Shan](/wiki/Tien-Shan "Tien-Shan"), and the [Chui River](/wiki/Chui_River "Chui River") valley (see map). The Oguz political association developed in the 9th and 10th centuries in the Syr Darya basin.[[205]](#cite_note-212)

#### Salar Oghuz migration

The [Salars](/wiki/Salar_people "Salar people") are descended from Turkmen who migrated from Central Asia and settled in a Tibetan area of Qinghai under Ming Chinese rule. The Salar ethnicity formed and underwent ethnogenesis from a process of male Turkmen migrants from Central Asia marrying [Amdo](/wiki/Amdo "Amdo") [Tibetan](/wiki/Tibetan_people "Tibetan people") women during the early Ming dynasty.[[206]](#cite_note-213)[[207]](#cite_note-214)[[208]](#cite_note-215)[[209]](#cite_note-216)

### Iranian, Indian, Arabic, and Anatolian expansion

Main articles: [Ghaznavids](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids"), [Seljuk Empire](/wiki/Seljuk_Empire "Seljuk Empire"), [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate"), [Mamluk Sultanate](/wiki/Mamluk_Sultanate "Mamluk Sultanate"), [Timurid Empire](/wiki/Timurid_Empire "Timurid Empire"), [Bahri dynasty](/wiki/Bahri_dynasty "Bahri dynasty"), [Deccan sultanates](/wiki/Deccan_sultanates "Deccan sultanates"), [Safavid Iran](/wiki/Safavid_Iran "Safavid Iran"), [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire"), [Afsharid dynasty](/wiki/Afsharid_dynasty "Afsharid dynasty"), and [Qizilbash](/wiki/Qizilbash "Qizilbash")

Turkic peoples and related groups migrated west from present-day [Northeastern China](/wiki/Northeastern_China "Northeastern China"), [Mongolia](/wiki/Mongolia "Mongolia"), [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia") and the [Turkestan](/wiki/Turkestan "Turkestan")-region towards the [Iranian plateau](/wiki/Iranian_plateau "Iranian plateau"), South Asia, and [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia") (modern Turkey) in many waves. The date of the initial expansion remains unknown.

#### Persia

##### Ghaznavid dynasty (977–1186)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Ghaznavid_Empire_975_-_1187_%28AD%29.PNG/250px-Ghaznavid_Empire_975_-_1187_%28AD%29.PNG)](/wiki/File:Ghaznavid_Empire_975_-_1187_(AD).PNG)

Ghaznavid Empire at its greatest extent in 1030 CE

Main articles: [Ghaznavids](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids") and [Uzbeks](/wiki/Uzbeks "Uzbeks")

The **Ghaznavid dynasty** ([Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"): غزنویان *ġaznaviyān*) was a [Persianate](/wiki/Persianate "Persianate")[[210]](#cite_note-217) [Muslim](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim") dynasty of Turkic *[mamluk](/wiki/Mamluk "Mamluk")* origin,[[211]](#cite_note-218) at their greatest extent ruling large parts of [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), much of [Transoxiana](/wiki/Transoxiana "Transoxiana") and the northwest [Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Indian_subcontinent "Indian subcontinent") (part of [Pakistan](/wiki/Pakistan "Pakistan")) from 977 to 1186.[[212]](#cite_note-EIr-219)[[213]](#cite_note-220)[[214]](#cite_note-EIslam-221) The dynasty was founded by [Sabuktigin](/wiki/Sabuktigin "Sabuktigin") upon his succession to rule of the region of [Ghazna](/wiki/Ghazni_Province "Ghazni Province") after the death of his father-in-law, [Alp Tigin](/wiki/Alp_Tigin "Alp Tigin"), who was a breakaway ex-general of the [Samanid Empire](/wiki/Samanid_Empire "Samanid Empire") from [Balkh](/wiki/Balkh "Balkh"), north of the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") in [Greater Khorasan](/wiki/Greater_Khorasan "Greater Khorasan").[[215]](#cite_note-EB-222)

Although the dynasty was of [Central Asian](/wiki/Central_Asian "Central Asian") Turkic origin, it was thoroughly [Persianised](/wiki/Persianised "Persianised") in terms of language, culture, literature and habits[[216]](#cite_note-223)[[217]](#cite_note-224)[[218]](#cite_note-225)[[219]](#cite_note-226) and hence is regarded by some as a "Persian dynasty".[[220]](#cite_note-227)

##### Seljuk Empire (1037–1194)

Main article: [Seljuk Empire](/wiki/Seljuk_Empire "Seljuk Empire")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c3/Seljuk_Empire_locator_map.svg/250px-Seljuk_Empire_locator_map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Seljuk_Empire_locator_map.svg)

A map showing the Seljuk Empire at its height, upon the death of Malik Shah I in 1092.

The **Seljuk Empire** ([Persian](/wiki/Persian_language "Persian language"): آل سلجوق, [romanized](/wiki/Romanization_of_Persian "Romanization of Persian"): *Āl-e Saljuq*, [lit.](/wiki/Literal_translation "Literal translation") 'House of Saljuq') or the **Great Seljuq Empire**[[221]](#cite_note-228)[[222]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEMecit2014128-229)[[223]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEPeacockYıldız20136-230) was a [high medieval](/wiki/High_medieval "High medieval") [Turko-Persian](/wiki/Turko-Persian "Turko-Persian")[[224]](#cite_note-231) [Sunni Muslim](/wiki/Sunni_Muslim "Sunni Muslim") [empire](/wiki/Empire "Empire"), originating from the [Qiniq](/wiki/K%C4%B1n%C4%B1k_(tribe) "Kınık (tribe)") branch of [Oghuz Turks](/wiki/Oghuz_Turks "Oghuz Turks").[[225]](#cite_note-232) At its greatest extent, the Seljuk Empire controlled a vast area stretching from western [Anatolia](/wiki/Anatolia "Anatolia") and the [Levant](/wiki/Levant "Levant") to the [Hindu Kush](/wiki/Hindu_Kush "Hindu Kush") in the east, and from [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") to the [Persian Gulf](/wiki/Persian_Gulf "Persian Gulf") in the south.

The Seljuk empire was founded by [Tughril Beg](/wiki/Tughril_Beg "Tughril Beg") (1016–1063) and his brother [Chaghri Beg](/wiki/Chaghri_Beg "Chaghri Beg") (989–1060) in 1037. From their homelands near the [Aral Sea](/wiki/Aral_Sea "Aral Sea"), the Seljuks advanced first into [Khorasan](/wiki/Khorasan "Khorasan") and then into mainland [Persia](/wiki/Persia "Persia"), before eventually conquering eastern Anatolia. Here the Seljuks won the [battle of Manzikert](/wiki/Battle_of_Manzikert "Battle of Manzikert") in 1071 and conquered most of Anatolia from the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire"), which became one of the reasons for the [First Crusade](/wiki/First_Crusade "First Crusade") (1095–1099). From c. 1150–1250, the Seljuk empire declined, and was invaded by the [Mongols](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire") around 1260. The Mongols divided Anatolia into [emirates](/wiki/Emirate "Emirate"). Eventually one of these, the [Ottoman](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), would conquer the rest.[[226]](#cite_note-233)

##### Timurid Empire (1370–1507)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9e/Timur_Empire.jpg/250px-Timur_Empire.jpg)](/wiki/File:Timur_Empire.jpg)

Map of the Timurid Empire at its greatest extent under Timur.

Main article: [Timurid Empire](/wiki/Timurid_Empire "Timurid Empire")

The [Timurid Empire](/wiki/Timurid_Empire "Timurid Empire") was a Turko-Mongol empire founded in the late 14th century through military conquests led by [Timurlane](/wiki/Timurlane "Timurlane"). The establishment of a cosmopolitan empire was followed by the [Timurid Renaissance](/wiki/Timurid_Renaissance "Timurid Renaissance"), a period of local enrichment in [mathematics](/wiki/Mathematics "Mathematics"), [astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy "Astronomy"), [architecture](/wiki/Architecture "Architecture"), as well as newfound economic growth.[[227]](#cite_note-234) The cultural progress of the Timurid period ended as soon as the empire collapsed in the early 16th century, leaving many intellecuals and artists to turn elsewhere in search of employment.[[228]](#cite_note-235)

##### Central Asian khanates (1501–1920)

Main articles: [Khanate of Bukhara](/wiki/Khanate_of_Bukhara "Khanate of Bukhara"), [Khanate of Khiva](/wiki/Khanate_of_Khiva "Khanate of Khiva"), [Khanate of Kokand](/wiki/Khanate_of_Kokand "Khanate of Kokand"), and [Emirate of Bukhara](/wiki/Emirate_of_Bukhara "Emirate of Bukhara")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0d/Mongolia_in_1636.svg/250px-Mongolia_in_1636.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Mongolia_in_1636.svg)

Central Asia in 1636

The [Bukhara Khanate](/wiki/Bukhara_Khanate "Bukhara Khanate") was an Uzbek[[229]](#cite_note-236) state that existed from 1501 to 1785. The khanate was ruled by three dynasties of the [Shaybanids](/wiki/Shaybanids "Shaybanids"), Janids and the Uzbek dynasty of Mangits. In 1785, [Shahmurad](/wiki/Shahmurad "Shahmurad"), formalized the family's dynastic rule ([Manghit](/wiki/Manghit "Manghit") dynasty), and the khanate became the [Emirate of Bukhara](/wiki/Emirate_of_Bukhara "Emirate of Bukhara") (1785–1920).[[230]](#cite_note-237) In 1710, the Kokand Khanate (1710–1876) separated from the Bukhara Khanate. In 1511–1920, [Khwarazm](/wiki/Khwarazm "Khwarazm") (Khiva Khanate) was ruled by the Arabshahid dynasty and the Uzbek dynasty of Kungrats.[[231]](#cite_note-238)

##### Afsharid dynasty (1736–1796)

The [Afsharid dynasty](/wiki/Afsharid_dynasty "Afsharid dynasty") was named after the Turkic Afshar tribe to which they belonged. The Afshars had migrated from [Turkestan](/wiki/Turkestan "Turkestan") to [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan") in the 13th century. The dynasty was founded in 1736 by the military commander [Nader Shah](/wiki/Nader_Shah "Nader Shah") who deposed the last member of the [Safavid dynasty](/wiki/Safavid_dynasty "Safavid dynasty") and proclaimed himself King of [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"). Nader belonged to the Qereqlu branch of the Afshars.[[232]](#cite_note-239) During Nader's reign, Iran reached its greatest extent since the [Sassanid Empire](/wiki/Sassanid_Empire "Sassanid Empire").

##### Qajar dynasty (1789–1925)

The Qajar dynasty was created by the Turkic [Qajar tribe](/wiki/Qajars_(tribe) "Qajars (tribe)"), ruling over Iran from 1789 to 1925.[[233]](#cite_note-autogenerated1-240)[[234]](#cite_note-241) The Qajar family took full control of Iran in 1794, deposing [Lotf 'Ali Khan](/wiki/Lotf_%27Ali_Khan "Lotf 'Ali Khan"), the last [Shah](/wiki/Shah "Shah") of the [Zand dynasty](/wiki/Zand_dynasty "Zand dynasty"), and re-asserted Iranian sovereignty over large parts of the [Caucasus](/wiki/Caucasus "Caucasus"). In 1796, [Mohammad Khan Qajar](/wiki/Mohammad_Khan_Qajar "Mohammad Khan Qajar") seized [Mashhad](/wiki/Mashhad "Mashhad") with ease,[[235]](#cite_note-242) putting an end to the [Afsharid dynasty](/wiki/Afsharid_dynasty "Afsharid dynasty"), and Mohammad Khan was formally crowned as Shah after his [punitive campaign against Iran's Georgian subjects](/wiki/Battle_of_Krtsanisi "Battle of Krtsanisi").[[236]](#cite_note-books.google.nl-243) In the Caucasus, the Qajar dynasty permanently lost many of Iran's integral areas[[237]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEFisherAveryHamblyMelville1991330-244) to the [Russians](/wiki/Imperial_Russia "Imperial Russia") over the course of the 19th century, comprising modern-day [Georgia](/wiki/Georgia_(country) "Georgia (country)"), [Dagestan](/wiki/Dagestan "Dagestan"), [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan") and [Armenia](/wiki/Armenia "Armenia").[[238]](#cite_note-Timothy_C._Dowling_pp_729-245) The dynasty was founded by [Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar](/wiki/Agha_Mohammad_Khan_Qajar "Agha Mohammad Khan Qajar") and continued until [Ahmad Shah Qajar](/wiki/Ahmad_Shah_Qajar "Ahmad Shah Qajar").

#### South Asia

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f7/The_Padshahnama%2C_Jahangir_presents_Prince_Khuram_with_a_turban_ornament.jpg/250px-The_Padshahnama%2C_Jahangir_presents_Prince_Khuram_with_a_turban_ornament.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Padshahnama,_Jahangir_presents_Prince_Khuram_with_a_turban_ornament.jpg)

[Mughal Emperor](/wiki/Mughal_Emperor "Mughal Emperor") [Jahangir](/wiki/Jahangir "Jahangir") presents [Prince Khurram](/wiki/Prince_Khurram "Prince Khurram") with a turban ornament.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/32/Humayun_%28left%29_with_his_father_Babur_%28right%29%2C_painted_circa_1640_%28Late_Shah_Jahan_Album%29.jpg/250px-Humayun_%28left%29_with_his_father_Babur_%28right%29%2C_painted_circa_1640_%28Late_Shah_Jahan_Album%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Humayun_(left)_with_his_father_Babur_(right),_painted_circa_1640_(Late_Shah_Jahan_Album).jpg)

[Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur"), founder of the [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") and Mughal emperor [Humayun](/wiki/Humayun "Humayun").

The [Delhi Sultanate](/wiki/Delhi_Sultanate "Delhi Sultanate") is a term used to cover five short-lived, [Delhi](/wiki/Delhi "Delhi")-based kingdoms, two of which were of Turkic origins: the [Mamluk dynasty](/wiki/Mamluk_dynasty_(Delhi) "Mamluk dynasty (Delhi)") (1206–1290) and the [Tughlaq dynasty](/wiki/Tughlaq_dynasty "Tughlaq dynasty") (1320–1414). [Southern India](/wiki/Southern_India "Southern India") saw rise of the [Qutb Shahi dynasty](/wiki/Qutb_Shahi_dynasty "Qutb Shahi dynasty"), one of the [Deccan sultanates](/wiki/Deccan_sultanates "Deccan sultanates").
The [Mughal Empire](/wiki/Mughal_Empire "Mughal Empire") was a Turko-Mongol empire that, at its greatest territorial extent, ruled most of South Asia, including [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), Pakistan, India, [Bangladesh](/wiki/Bangladesh "Bangladesh") and parts of [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan") from the early 16th to the early 18th centuries. The Mughal dynasty was founded by a [Turko-Mongol](/wiki/Turko-Mongol "Turko-Mongol") prince named [Babur](/wiki/Babur "Babur") (reigned 1526–1530), who was descended from [Timur](/wiki/Timur "Timur") (Tamerlane) on his father's side and from Chagatai, second son of the [Mongol](/wiki/Mongol "Mongol") ruler [Genghis Khan](/wiki/Genghis_Khan "Genghis Khan"), on his mother's side.[[239]](#cite_note-Mughal_Dynasty-246)[[240]](#cite_note-247) A further distinction was the attempt of the Mughals to integrate Hindus and Muslims into a united Indian state.[[239]](#cite_note-Mughal_Dynasty-246)[[241]](#cite_note-248)[[242]](#cite_note-249)[[243]](#cite_note-250)

#### Arab world

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Dirham_of_al-Muttaqi.jpg/250px-Dirham_of_al-Muttaqi.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dirham_of_al-Muttaqi.jpg)

Silver *[dirham](/wiki/Dirham "Dirham")* of [AH](/wiki/Hijri_year "Hijri year") 329 (940/941 CE), with the names of Caliph [al-Muttaqi](/wiki/Al-Muttaqi "Al-Muttaqi") and Amir al-umara [Bajkam](/wiki/Bajkam "Bajkam") (de facto ruler of the country)

The Arab Muslim [Umayyads](/wiki/Umayyad_Caliphate "Umayyad Caliphate") and [Abbasids](/wiki/Abbasids "Abbasids") fought against the pagan Turks in the [Türgesh](/wiki/T%C3%BCrgesh "Türgesh") Khaganate in the [Muslim conquest of Transoxiana](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_of_Transoxiana "Muslim conquest of Transoxiana"). Turkic soldiers in the army of the [Abbasid](/wiki/Abbasid "Abbasid") [caliphs](/wiki/Caliphs "Caliphs") emerged as the de facto rulers of most of the Muslim Middle East (apart from [Syria](/wiki/Syria "Syria") and [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt")), particularly after the 10th century. Examples of regional de facto independent states include the short lived [Tulunids](/wiki/Tulunids "Tulunids") and [Ikhshidids](/wiki/Ikhshidids "Ikhshidids") in Egypt. The [Oghuz](/wiki/Oghuz_Turks "Oghuz Turks") and other tribes captured and dominated various countries under the leadership of the [Seljuk dynasty](/wiki/Seljuk_dynasty "Seljuk dynasty") and eventually captured the territories of the Abbasid dynasty and the [Byzantine Empire](/wiki/Byzantine_Empire "Byzantine Empire").[[168]](#cite_note-Carter_V._Findley-174)

#### Anatolia – Ottomans

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/06/OttomanEmpireIn1683.png/250px-OttomanEmpireIn1683.png)](/wiki/File:OttomanEmpireIn1683.png)

Ottoman empire in 1683

After many battles, the western [Oghuz Turks](/wiki/Oghuz_Turks "Oghuz Turks") established their own state and later constructed the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"). The main migration of the Oghuz Turks occurred in medieval times, when they spread across most of Asia and into Europe and the Middle East.[[168]](#cite_note-Carter_V._Findley-174) They also took part in the military encounters of the [Crusades](/wiki/Crusades "Crusades").[[244]](#cite_note-251) In 1090–91, the Turkic Pechenegs reached the walls of [Constantinople](/wiki/Constantinople "Constantinople"), where Emperor [Alexius I](/wiki/Alexius_I "Alexius I") with the aid of the Kipchaks annihilated their army.[[245]](#cite_note-252)

As the [Seljuk Empire](/wiki/Seljuk_Empire "Seljuk Empire") declined following the [Mongol invasion](/wiki/Mongol_invasion "Mongol invasion"), the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire") emerged as the new important Turkic state, that came to dominate not only the Middle East, but even southeastern Europe, parts of southwestern Russia, and northern Africa.[[168]](#cite_note-Carter_V._Findley-174)

#### Islamization

Turkic peoples like the [Karluks](/wiki/Karluks "Karluks") (mainly 8th century), [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghur_Khaganate#Successors "Uyghur Khaganate"), [Kyrgyz](/wiki/Kyrgyz_people "Kyrgyz people"), [Turkmens](/wiki/Turkmens "Turkmens"), and [Kipchaks](/wiki/Kipchaks "Kipchaks") later came into contact with [Muslims](/wiki/Muslim "Muslim"), and most of them gradually adopted [Islam](/wiki/Islam "Islam"). Some groups of Turkic people practice other religions, including their original animistic-shamanistic religion, [Christianity](/wiki/Christianity "Christianity"), [Burkhanism](/wiki/Burkhanism "Burkhanism"), [Judaism](/wiki/Judaism "Judaism") ([Khazars](/wiki/Khazars "Khazars"), [Krymchaks](/wiki/Krymchaks "Krymchaks"), [Crimean Karaites](/wiki/Crimean_Karaites "Crimean Karaites")), [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism"), and a small number of [Zoroastrians](/wiki/Zoroastrians "Zoroastrians").

### Modern history

[![Map highlighting present-day Turkic countries](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/Map_of_Independent_Turkic_countries.svg/500px-Map_of_Independent_Turkic_countries.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map_of_Independent_Turkic_countries.svg)

Independent Turkic states shown in red

The Ottoman Empire gradually grew weaker in the face of poor administration, repeated wars with [Russia](/wiki/Imperial_Russia "Imperial Russia"), Austria and Hungary, and the [emergence of nationalist movements in the Balkans](/wiki/Emergence_of_nationalist_movements_in_the_Balkans "Emergence of nationalist movements in the Balkans"), and it finally gave way after World War I to the present-day [Republic of Turkey](/wiki/Republic_of_Turkey "Republic of Turkey").[[168]](#cite_note-Carter_V._Findley-174)
Ethnic nationalism also developed in Ottoman Empire during the 19th century, taking the form of [Pan-Turkism](/wiki/Pan-Turkism "Pan-Turkism") or [Turanism](/wiki/Turanism "Turanism").

The Turkic peoples of Central Asia were not organized in nation-states during most of the 20th century, after the collapse of the [Russian Empire](/wiki/Russian_Empire "Russian Empire") living either in the Soviet Union or (after a short-lived [First East Turkestan Republic](/wiki/First_East_Turkestan_Republic "First East Turkestan Republic")) in the [Chinese Republic](/wiki/Republic_of_China_(1912%E2%80%9349) "Republic of China (1912–49)"). For much of the 20th century, Turkey was the only independent Turkic country.[[246]](#cite_note-253)

In 1991, after the disintegration of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), five Turkic states gained their independence. These were [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan"), [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"), [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan "Kyrgyzstan"), [Turkmenistan](/wiki/Turkmenistan "Turkmenistan"), and [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"). Other Turkic regions such as [Tatarstan](/wiki/Tatarstan "Tatarstan"), [Tuva](/wiki/Tuva "Tuva"), and [Yakutia](/wiki/Yakutia "Yakutia") remained in the [Russian Federation](/wiki/Russian_Federation "Russian Federation"). [Chinese Turkestan](/wiki/Chinese_Turkestan "Chinese Turkestan") remained part of the [People's Republic of China](/wiki/People%27s_Republic_of_China "People's Republic of China"). Immediately after the independence of the Turkic states, Turkey began seeking diplomatic relations with them. Over time political meetings between the Turkic countries increased and led to the establishment of [TÜRKSOY](/wiki/T%C3%9CRKSOY "TÜRKSOY") in 1993 and the [Turkic Council](/wiki/Turkic_Council "Turkic Council") in 2009, which later was renamed [Organization of Turkic States](/wiki/Organization_of_Turkic_States "Organization of Turkic States") in 2021.[[247]](#cite_note-254)

## Physiognomy

According to historians Joo-Yup Lee and Shuntu Kuang, Chinese official histories do not depict Turkic peoples as belonging to a single uniform entity called "Turks".[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017228-255) However "Chinese histories also depict the Turkic-speaking peoples as typically possessing East/Inner Asian [physiognomy](/wiki/Physiognomy "Physiognomy"), as well as occasionally having West Eurasian physiognomy."[[248]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017228-255) According to "fragmentary information on the Xiongnu language that can be found in the Chinese histories, the Xiongnu were Turkic",[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017199-256) however historians have been unable to confirm whether or not they were Turkic. [Sima Qian](/wiki/Sima_Qian "Sima Qian")'s description of their legendary origins suggest their physiognomy was "not too different from that of... Han (漢) Chinese population",[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017199-256) but a subset of Xiongnu known as the [Jie people](/wiki/Jie_people "Jie people") were described having "deep-set eyes", "high nose bridges" and "heavy facial hair".[[249]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017199-256) The Jie may have been [Yeniseian](/wiki/Yeniseian_people "Yeniseian people"), although others maintaining an [Iranian](/wiki/Iranian_peoples "Iranian peoples") affiliation, and regardless of whether or not the Xiongnu were Turkic, they were a hybrid people.[[250]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017228-229-257) According to the *[Old Book of Tang](/wiki/Old_Book_of_Tang "Old Book of Tang")*, [Ashina Simo](/wiki/Ashina_Simo "Ashina Simo") "was not given a high military post by the Ashina rulers because of his Sogdian (*huren* 胡人) physiognomy."[[251]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017201-258) The [Tang](/wiki/Tang_dynasty "Tang dynasty") historian [Yan Shigu](/wiki/Yan_Shigu "Yan Shigu") described the Hu people of his day as "blue-eyed and red bearded"[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017202-259) descendants of the [Wusun](/wiki/Wusun "Wusun"), whereas "no comparable depiction of the Kök Türks or Tiele is found in the official Chinese histories."[[252]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017202-259)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a4/Turk_Shahi_portrait._King_Sri_Ranasrikari._Late_7th_to_early_8th_century_CE.jpg/250px-Turk_Shahi_portrait._King_Sri_Ranasrikari._Late_7th_to_early_8th_century_CE.jpg)](/wiki/File:Turk_Shahi_portrait._King_Sri_Ranasrikari._Late_7th_to_early_8th_century_CE.jpg)

An early [Turk Shahi](/wiki/Turk_Shahi "Turk Shahi") ruler named Sri Ranasrikari "The Lord who brings excellence through war" ([Brahmi script](/wiki/Brahmi_script "Brahmi script")). In this realistic portrait, he wears the Turkic double-lapel [caftan](/wiki/Caftan "Caftan"). Late 7th to early 8th century CE.[[253]](#cite_note-260)[[254]](#cite_note-261)[[255]](#cite_note-KHM13-262)

Historian [Peter Golden](/wiki/Peter_Benjamin_Golden "Peter Benjamin Golden") has reported that genetic testing of the proposed descendants of the Ashina tribe does seem to confirm a link to the [Indo-Iranians](/wiki/Indo-Iranians "Indo-Iranians"), emphasizing that "*the Turks as a whole 'were made up of heterogeneous and somatically dissimilar populations'*".[[256]](#cite_note-263) Historian [Emel Esin](https://tr.wikipedia.org/wiki/Emel_Esin "tr:Emel Esin") and Professor [Xue Zongzheng](/wiki/Xue_Zongzheng "Xue Zongzheng") have argued that West Eurasian features were typical of the royal [Ashina clan](/wiki/Ashina_tribe "Ashina tribe") of the Eastern Turkic Khaganate and that their appearance shifted to an East Asian one due to intermarriage with foreign nobility. As a result, by the time of [Kul Tigin](/wiki/Bust_of_Kul_Tigin "Bust of Kul Tigin") (684 AD), members of the Ashina dynasty had East Asian features.[[257]](#cite_note-Wang2018-264)[[258]](#cite_note-Esin1980-265) A 2023 genetic study found that [Empress Ashina](/wiki/Empress_Ashina "Empress Ashina") (568–578 AD), a Royal Göktürk, had nearly entirely [Ancient Northeast Asian](/wiki/Ancient_Northeast_Asian "Ancient Northeast Asian") origin, weakening the "western Eurasian origin and multiple origin hypotheses".[[121]](#cite_note-Yang_2023-127) Lee and Kuang believe it is likely "early and medieval Turkic peoples themselves did not form a homogeneous entity and that some of them, non-Turkic by origin, had become Turkicised at some point in history."[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017226-266) They also suggest that many modern Turkic-speaking populations are not directly descended from early Turkic peoples.[[259]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017226-266) Lee and Kuang concluded that "both medieval Chinese histories and modern DNA studies point to the fact that the early and medieval Turkic peoples were made up of heterogeneous and somatically dissimilar populations."[[260]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017198-267)

Like Chinese historians, Medieval Muslim writers generally depicted the Turks as having an East Asian appearance.[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017207-268) Unlike Chinese historians, Medieval Muslim writers used the term "Turk" broadly to refer to not only Turkic-speaking peoples but also various non-Turkic speaking peoples,[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017207-268) such as the [Hephthalites](/wiki/Hephthalites "Hephthalites"), [Rus](/wiki/Rus%27_people "Rus' people"), [Magyars](/wiki/Magyars "Magyars"), and [Tibetans](/wiki/Tibetans "Tibetans"). In the 13th century, [Juzjani](/wiki/Minhaj-i_Siraj_Juzjani "Minhaj-i Siraj Juzjani") referred to the people of Tibet and the mountains between Tibet and Bengal as "Turks" and "people with Turkish features."[[262]](#cite_note-Wink2002-269) Medieval Arab and Persian descriptions of Turks state that they looked strange from their perspective and were extremely physically different from Arabs. Turks were described as "broad faced people with small eyes", having light-colored, often reddish hair, and with pink skin,[[263]](#cite_note-AmitaiBiran2005-270) as being "short, with small eyes, nostrils, and mouths" ([Sharaf al-Zaman al-Marwazi](/wiki/Sharaf_al-Zaman_al-Marwazi "Sharaf al-Zaman al-Marwazi")), as being "full-faced with small eyes" ([Al-Tabari](/wiki/Al-Tabari "Al-Tabari")), as possessing "a large head (*sar-i buzurg*), a broad face (*rūy-i pahn*), narrow eyes (*chashmhā-i tang*), and a flat nose (*bīnī-i pakhch*), and unpleasing lips and teeth (*lab va dandān na nīkū*)" ([Keikavus](/wiki/Keikavus_(Ziyarid) "Keikavus (Ziyarid)")).[[264]](#cite_note-Lee_&_Kuang_2017-271) On [Western Turkic](/wiki/Western_Turkic_Khaganate "Western Turkic Khaganate") coins "the faces of the governor and governess are clearly Mongoloid (a roundish face, narrow eyes), and the portrait have definite old Türk features (long hair, absence of headdress of the governor, a tricorn headdress of the governess)".[[265]](#cite_note-Babayar2013-272)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Portrait_from_the_Palace_courtroom%2C_Lashkari_Bazar.jpg/250px-Portrait_from_the_Palace_courtroom%2C_Lashkari_Bazar.jpg)](/wiki/File:Portrait_from_the_Palace_courtroom,_Lashkari_Bazar.jpg)

[Ghaznavid](/wiki/Ghaznavid "Ghaznavid") portrait, Palace of [Lashkari Bazar](/wiki/Lashkari_Bazar "Lashkari Bazar"). [Schlumberger](/wiki/Daniel_Schlumberger "Daniel Schlumberger") noted that the [turban](/wiki/Turban "Turban"), the small mouth and the strongly slanted eyes were characteristically Turkic.[[266]](#cite_note-jstor.org-273)

In the [Ghaznavids](/wiki/Ghaznavids "Ghaznavids")' residential palace of [Lashkari Bazar](/wiki/Lashkari_Bazar "Lashkari Bazar"), there survives a partially conserved portrait depicting a turbaned and haloed adolescent figure with full cheeks, slanted eyes, and a small, sinuous mouth.[[266]](#cite_note-jstor.org-273) The Armenian historian [Movses Kaghankatvatsi](/wiki/Movses_Kaghankatvatsi "Movses Kaghankatvatsi") describes the Turks of the [Western Turkic Khaganate](/wiki/Western_Turkic_Khaganate "Western Turkic Khaganate") as "broad-faced, without eyelashes, and with long flowing hair like women".[[267]](#cite_note-274)

[Al-Masudi](/wiki/Al-Masudi "Al-Masudi") writes that the [Oghuz Turks](/wiki/Oghuz_Turks "Oghuz Turks") in Yengi-kent near the mouth of the [Syr Darya](/wiki/Syr_Darya "Syr Darya") "are distinguished from other Turks by their valour, their slanted eyes, and the smallness of their stature."[[261]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017207-268) Later Muslim writers noted a change in the physiognomy of Oghuz Turks. According to [Rashid al-Din Hamadani](/wiki/Rashid_al-Din_Hamadani "Rashid al-Din Hamadani"), "because of the climate their features gradually changed into those of Tajiks. Since they were not Tajiks, the Tajik peoples called them *turkmān*, i.e. Turk-like (*Turk-mānand*)." Ḥāfiẓ Tanīsh Mīr Muḥammad Bukhārī also related that the Oghuz' 'Turkic face did not remain as it was' after their migration into [Transoxiana](/wiki/Transoxiana "Transoxiana") and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"). [Khiva](/wiki/Khanate_of_Khiva "Khanate of Khiva") khan [Abu al-Ghazi Bahadur](/wiki/Abu_al-Ghazi_Bahadur "Abu al-Ghazi Bahadur") wrote in his [Chagatai language](/wiki/Chagatai_language "Chagatai language") treatise *[Shajara-i Tarākima](/wiki/Shajara-i_Tar%C4%81kima "Shajara-i Tarākima")* (Genealogy of the Turkmens) that "their chin started to become narrow, their eyes started to become large, their faces started to become small, and their noses started to become big' after five or six generations". Ottoman historian [Mustafa Âlî](/wiki/Mustafa_%C3%82l%C3%AE "Mustafa Âlî") commented in *Künhüʾl-aḫbār* that Anatolian Turks and Ottoman elites are ethnically mixed: "Most of the inhabitants of [Rûm](/wiki/R%C3%BBm "Rûm") are of confused ethnic origin. Among its notables there are few whose lineage does not go back to a convert to Islam."[[268]](#cite_note-275)

Kevin Alan Brook states that like "most nomadic Turks, the Western Turkic [Khazars](/wiki/Khazars "Khazars") were racially and ethnically mixed."[[269]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrook20183-276) [Istakhri](/wiki/Istakhri "Istakhri") described Khazars as having black hair while [Ibn Sa'id al-Maghribi](/wiki/Ibn_Sa%27id_al-Maghribi "Ibn Sa'id al-Maghribi") described them as having blue eyes, light skin, and reddish hair. Istakhri mentions that there were "Black Khazars" and "White Khazars." Most scholars believe these were political designations: black being lower class while white being higher class. Constantin Zuckerman argues that these "had physical and racial differences and explained that they stemmed from the merger of the Khazars with the Barsils."[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrook20184-277) [Old East Slavic](/wiki/Old_East_Slavic "Old East Slavic") sources called the Khazars the "White Ugry" and the Magyars the "Black Ugry."[[271]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrook20185-278) Soviet excavated Khazar remains show Slavic-type, European-type, and a minority Mongoloid-type skulls.[[270]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrook20184-277)

The [Yenisei Kyrgyz](/wiki/Yenisei_Kyrgyz "Yenisei Kyrgyz") are mentioned in the *[New Book of Tang](/wiki/New_Book_of_Tang "New Book of Tang")* as having the same script and language as the [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghur_Khaganate "Uyghur Khaganate") but "The people are all tall and
big and have red hair, white faces, and green eyes."[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017204-279)[[note 2]](#cite_note-284) The *New Book of Tang* also states that the neighboring Boma tribe resembled the Kyrgyz but their language was different, which may imply the Kyrgyz were originally a non-Turkic people, who were later Turkicized through inter-tribal marriages.[[272]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017204-279) According to [Gardizi](/wiki/Gardizi "Gardizi"), the Kyrgyz were mixed with "Saqlabs" (Slavs), which explains the red hair and white skin among the Kyrgyz, while the *New Book* states that the Kyrgyz "intermixed with the Dingling."[[277]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTEBrook20183-4-285)[[278]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017204-205-286) The Kyrgyz "regarded those with black eyes as descending from [Li] Ling," a [Han dynasty](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") general who defected to the Xiongnu.[[279]](#cite_note-FOOTNOTELeeKuang2017215-287)

In a Chinese legal statute from the early period of the [Ming dynasty](/wiki/Ming_dynasty "Ming dynasty"), the [Kipchaks](/wiki/Kipchaks "Kipchaks") are described as having blond hair and [blue](/wiki/Blue "Blue") eyes. It also states that they had a "vile" and "peculiar" appearance, and that some Chinese people would not want to marry them.[[280]](#cite_note-288)[[281]](#cite_note-289) Russian anthropologist Oshanin (1964: 24, 32) notes that "the 'Mongoloid' phenotype, characteristic of modern Kazakhs and Qirghiz, prevails among the skulls of the Qipchaq and Pecheneg nomads found in the kurgans in eastern Ukraine"; Lee & Kuang (2017) propose that Oshanin's discovery is explainable by assuming that the historical Kipchaks' modern descendants are [Kazakhs](/wiki/Kazakhs "Kazakhs") of the [Lesser Horde](/wiki/Zhuz#Junior_zhuz "Zhuz"), whose men possess a high frequency of haplogroup C2's subclade C2b1b1 (59.7 to 78%). Lee and Kuang also suggest that the high frequency (63.9%) of the Y-DNA haplogroup R-M73 among Karakypshaks (a tribe within the Kipchaks) allows inference about the genetics of Karakypshaks' medieval ancestors, thus explaining why some medieval Kipchaks were described as possessing "blue [or green] eyes and red hair.[[282]](#cite_note-290)

Byzantine historians of the 11th-12th centuries provided description of Turkmens as very different from the Greeks. [Bertrandon de la Broquière](/wiki/Bertrandon_de_la_Broqui%C3%A8re "Bertrandon de la Broquière"), a French traveller to the [Ottoman Empire](/wiki/Ottoman_Empire "Ottoman Empire"), met with sultan [Murad II](/wiki/Murad_II "Murad II") in [Adrianople](/wiki/Adrianople "Adrianople"), and described him in the following terms: "In the first place, as I have seen him frequently, I shall say that he is a little, short, thick man, with the physiognomy of a [Tartar](/wiki/Tatars "Tatars"). He has a broad and brown face, high cheek bones, a round beard, a great and crooked nose, with little eyes".[[283]](#cite_note-291)

### Remarks

1. **[^](#cite_ref-205)** For its etymology see [Kangar union#Etymology](/wiki/Kangar_union#Etymology "Kangar union")
2. **[^](#cite_ref-284)** 9th-century author [Duan Chengshi](/wiki/Duan_Chengshi "Duan Chengshi") described the Kyrgyz tribe (*Jiankun buluo* 堅昆部落) as "yellow-haired, green-eyed, red-mustached [and red-]bearded".[[273]](#cite_note-280) [New Book of Tang](/wiki/New_Book_of_Tang "New Book of Tang") (finished in 1060) describes [Alats](/wiki/Alat_tribe "Alat tribe"), a medieval Turkic people, as resembling Kyrgyz[[274]](#cite_note-281) who were "all tall, red-haired, pale-faced, green-irised";[[275]](#cite_note-282) New Book of Tang also states that Kyrgyz regarded black hair as "infelicitous" and insisted that black-eyed individuals were descendants of [Han](/wiki/Han_dynasty "Han dynasty") general [Li Ling](/wiki/Li_Ling "Li Ling").[[276]](#cite_note-283)

## International organizations

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a9/Map-TurksoyMembers.svg/250px-Map-TurksoyMembers.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Map-TurksoyMembers.svg)

Map of [TÜRKSOY](/wiki/Joint_Administration_of_Turkic_Arts_and_Culture "Joint Administration of Turkic Arts and Culture") members.

Further information: [Pan-Turkism](/wiki/Pan-Turkism "Pan-Turkism")

There are several international organizations created with the purpose of furthering cooperation between countries with Turkic-speaking populations, such as the [Joint Administration of Turkic Arts and Culture](/wiki/Joint_Administration_of_Turkic_Arts_and_Culture "Joint Administration of Turkic Arts and Culture") (TÜRKSOY) and the Parliamentary Assembly of Turkic-speaking Countries (TÜRKPA) and the [Turkic Council](/wiki/Turkic_Council "Turkic Council").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/76/Conseilturcique.svg/250px-Conseilturcique.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Conseilturcique.svg)

Members

Observer States

The [TAKM](/wiki/TAKM "TAKM") – Organization of the Eurasian Law Enforcement Agencies with Military Status, was established on 25 January 2013. It is an [intergovernmental](/wiki/Intergovernmental_organization "Intergovernmental organization") [military](/wiki/Military "Military") [law enforcement](/wiki/Law_enforcement "Law enforcement") ([gendarmerie](/wiki/Gendarmerie "Gendarmerie")) organization of currently three Turkic countries ([Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan"), [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan "Kyrgyzstan") and [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey")) and [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan") as observer.

### TÜRKSOY

Türksoy carries out activities to strengthen cultural ties between Turkic peoples. One of the main goals to transmit their common cultural heritage to future generations and promote it around the world.[[284]](#cite_note-:0-292)

Every year, one city in the Turkic world is selected as the "Cultural Capital of the Turkic World". Within the framework of events to celebrate the Cultural Capital of the Turkic World, numerous cultural events are held, gathering artists, scholars and intellectuals, giving them the opportunity to exchange their experiences, as well as promoting the city in question internationally.[[285]](#cite_note-:1-293)

### Organization of Turkic States

The [Organization of Turkic States](/wiki/Organization_of_Turkic_States "Organization of Turkic States"), founded on 3 November 2009, by the *Nakhchivan Agreement* confederation, [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"), [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan "Kyrgyzstan") and [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey"), aims to integrate these organizations into a tighter geopolitical framework.

The member countries are [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan"), [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"), [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan "Kyrgyzstan"), [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan").[[286]](#cite_note-294) The idea of setting up this cooperative council was first put forward by Kazakh President [Nursultan Nazarbayev](/wiki/Nursultan_Nazarbayev "Nursultan Nazarbayev") back in 2006. [Hungary](/wiki/Hungary "Hungary") has announced to be interested in joining the Organization of Turkic States. Since August 2018, Hungary has official observer status in the Organization of Turkic States.[[287]](#cite_note-295) [Turkmenistan](/wiki/Turkmenistan "Turkmenistan") also joined as an observer state to the organization at 8th summit.[[288]](#cite_note-296) [Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus](/wiki/Turkish_Republic_of_Northern_Cyprus "Turkish Republic of Northern Cyprus") was admitted to the organization as observer member at the [2022 Samarkand Summit](/wiki/2022_Organization_of_Turkic_States_summit "2022 Organization of Turkic States summit").[[289]](#cite_note-297)[[290]](#cite_note-298)

## Demographics

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/70/Bashkir_in_Paris.jpg/250px-Bashkir_in_Paris.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bashkir_in_Paris.jpg)

[Bashkirs](/wiki/Bashkirs "Bashkirs"), painting from 1812, Paris

The distribution of people of Turkic cultural background ranges from [Siberia](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"), across Central Asia, to Southern Europe. As of 2011[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Turkic_peoples&action=edit) the largest groups of Turkic people live throughout Central Asia—[Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"), [Kyrgyzstan](/wiki/Kyrgyzstan "Kyrgyzstan"), [Turkmenistan](/wiki/Turkmenistan "Turkmenistan"), [Uzbekistan](/wiki/Uzbekistan "Uzbekistan"), and [Azerbaijan](/wiki/Azerbaijan "Azerbaijan"), in addition to [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey") and [Iran](/wiki/Iran "Iran"). Additionally, Turkic people are found within [Crimea](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea"), [Altishahr](/wiki/Altishahr "Altishahr") region of western [China](/wiki/China "China"), northern [Iraq](/wiki/Iraq "Iraq"), [Israel](/wiki/Israel "Israel"), [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia"), [Afghanistan](/wiki/Afghanistan "Afghanistan"), [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus"), and the [Balkans](/wiki/Balkans "Balkans"): [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"), [Bulgaria](/wiki/Bulgaria "Bulgaria"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece") and former [Yugoslavia](/wiki/Yugoslavia "Yugoslavia").

A small number of Turkic people also live in [Vilnius](/wiki/Vilnius "Vilnius"), the capital of [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"). Small numbers inhabit eastern [Poland](/wiki/Poland "Poland") and the south-eastern part of [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland").[[291]](#cite_note-299) There are also considerable populations of Turkic people (originating mostly from [Turkey](/wiki/Turkey "Turkey")) in [Germany](/wiki/Germany "Germany"), [United States](/wiki/United_States "United States"), and [Australia](/wiki/Australia "Australia"), largely because of migrations during the 20th century.

Sometimes ethnographers group Turkic people into six branches: the [Oghuz Turks](/wiki/Oghuz_Turks "Oghuz Turks"), [Kipchak](/wiki/Kipchaks "Kipchaks"), [Karluk](/wiki/Karluks "Karluks"), [Siberian](/wiki/Siberia "Siberia"), [Chuvash](/wiki/Chuvash_people "Chuvash people"), and [Sakha/Yakut](/wiki/Sakha_language "Sakha language") branches. The Oghuz have been termed Western Turks, while the remaining five, in such a classificatory scheme, are called Eastern Turks.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The genetic distances between the different populations of Uzbeks scattered across Uzbekistan is no greater than the distance between many of them and the Karakalpaks. This suggests that Karakalpaks and Uzbeks have very similar origins. The Karakalpaks have a somewhat greater bias towards the eastern markers than the Uzbeks.[[292]](#cite_note-300)

## Cuisine

Markets in the steppe region had a limited range of foodstuffs available—mostly [grains](/wiki/Grain "Grain"), [dried fruits](/wiki/Dried_fruit "Dried fruit"), [spices](/wiki/Spices "Spices"), and [tea](/wiki/Tea "Tea"). Turks mostly herded [sheep](/wiki/Sheep "Sheep"), [goats](/wiki/Goat "Goat") and [horses](/wiki/Horse "Horse"). Dairy was a staple of the nomadic diet and there are many Turkic words for various dairy products such as *süt* (milk), *yagh* (butter), [ayran](/wiki/Ayran "Ayran"), *[qaymaq](/wiki/Kaymak "Kaymak")* (similar to [clotted cream](/wiki/Clotted_cream "Clotted cream")), [qi̅mi̅z](/wiki/Kumis "Kumis") (fermented mare's milk) and *[qurut](/wiki/Qurut "Qurut")* (dried yoghurt). During the Middle Ages [Kazakh](/wiki/Kazakh_cuisine "Kazakh cuisine"), [Kyrgyz](/wiki/Kyrgyz_people "Kyrgyz people") and [Tatars](/wiki/Tatar_cuisine "Tatar cuisine"), who were historically part of the Turkic nomadic group known as the [Golden Horde](/wiki/Golden_Horde "Golden Horde"), continued to develop new variations of dairy products.[[295]](#cite_note-osfc-303)

Nomadic Turks cooked their meals in a *[qazan](/wiki/Kazan_(cookware) "Kazan (cookware)")*, a pot similar to a [cauldron](/wiki/Cauldron "Cauldron"); a wooden rack called a *qasqan* can be used to prepare certain steamed foods, like the traditional meat dumplings called *[manti](/wiki/Manti_(food) "Manti (food)")*. They also used a *saj*, a griddle that was traditionally placed on stones over a fire, and *[shish](/wiki/Skewer "Skewer")*. In later times, the Persian [tava](/wiki/Tava "Tava") was borrowed from the Persians for frying, but traditionally nomadic Turks did most of their cooking using the qazan, saj and shish. Meals were served in a bowl, called a *chanaq*, and eaten with a knife (*bïchaq*) and spoon (*qashi̅q*). Both bowl and spoon were historically made from wood. Other traditional utensils used in food preparation included a thin rolling pin called *oqlaghu*, a [colander](/wiki/Colander "Colander") called *süzgu̅çh*, and a grinding stone called *tāgirmān*.[[295]](#cite_note-osfc-303)

Medieval grain dishes included preparations of whole grains, soups, porridges, breads and pastries. Fried or toasted whole grains were called *qawïrmach*, while *köchä* was crushed grain that was cooked with dairy products. *Salma* were broad [noodles](/wiki/Noodles "Noodles") that could be served with boiled or roasted meat; cut noodles were called *tutmaj* in the Middle Ages and are called *[kesme](/wiki/Kesme "Kesme")* today.[[295]](#cite_note-osfc-303)

There are many types of bread doughs in Turkic cuisine. *[Yupqa](/wiki/Saj_bread "Saj bread")* is the thinnest type of dough, *[bawi̅rsaq](/wiki/Bawirsaq "Bawirsaq")* is a type of fried bread dough, and *[chälpäk](/wiki/Shelpek "Shelpek")* is a deep-fried flat bread. *[Qatlama](/wiki/Qatlama "Qatlama")* is a fried bread that may be sprinkled with dried fruit or meat, rolled, and sliced like [pinwheel sandwiches](/w/index.php?title=Pinwheel_sandwiches&action=edit&redlink=1 "Pinwheel sandwiches (page does not exist)"). *[Toqach](/wiki/Toqach "Toqach")* and *[chöräk](/w/index.php?title=Ch%C3%B6r%C3%A4k&action=edit&redlink=1 "Chöräk (page does not exist)")* are varieties of bread, and [böräk](/wiki/B%C3%B6rek "Börek") is a type of filled pie [pastry](/wiki/Pastry "Pastry").[[295]](#cite_note-osfc-303)

Herd animals were usually slaughtered during the winter months and various types of sausages were prepared to preserve the meats, including a type of sausage called *[sujuk](/wiki/Sujuk "Sujuk")*. Though prohibited by [Islamic dietary restrictions](/wiki/Halal "Halal"), historically Turkic nomads also had a variety of [blood sausage](/wiki/Blood_sausage "Blood sausage"). One type of sausage, called *[qazi̅](/wiki/Qaz%C4%B1 "Qazı")*, was made from horsemeat and another variety was filled with a mixture of ground meat, [offal](/wiki/Offal "Offal") and rice. Chopped meat was called *qïyma* and spit-roasted meat was *söklünch*—from the root *sök-* meaning "to tear off", the latter dish is known as [kebab](/wiki/Kebab "Kebab") in modern times. *[Qawirma](/wiki/Kavurma "Kavurma")* is a typical fried meat dish, and *[kullama](/wiki/Kullama "Kullama")* is a soup of noodles and lamb.[[295]](#cite_note-osfc-303)

## Religion

### Early Turkic mythology and Tengrism

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4f/Kyzyl_Shaman.jpg/120px-Kyzyl_Shaman.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kyzyl_Shaman.jpg)

A shaman doctor of [Kyzyl](/wiki/Kyzyl "Kyzyl").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d7/K%C3%B6r%C3%B6sf%C5%91i-Kriesch_Alad%C3%A1r_-_S%C3%A1m%C3%A1nok_k%C3%B6rt%C3%A1nca_1911_Marosv%C3%A1s%C3%A1rhely_Kulturpalota.png/250px-K%C3%B6r%C3%B6sf%C5%91i-Kriesch_Alad%C3%A1r_-_S%C3%A1m%C3%A1nok_k%C3%B6rt%C3%A1nca_1911_Marosv%C3%A1s%C3%A1rhely_Kulturpalota.png)](/wiki/File:K%C3%B6r%C3%B6sf%C5%91i-Kriesch_Alad%C3%A1r_-_S%C3%A1m%C3%A1nok_k%C3%B6rt%C3%A1nca_1911_Marosv%C3%A1s%C3%A1rhely_Kulturpalota.png)

[Circle dance](/wiki/Circle_dance "Circle dance") of Shamans 1911

Main articles: [Turkic mythology](/wiki/Turkic_mythology "Turkic mythology"), [Tengrism](/wiki/Tengrism "Tengrism"), and [Shamanism](/wiki/Shamanism "Shamanism")

Early [Turkic mythology](/wiki/Turkic_mythology "Turkic mythology") was dominated by [Shamanism](/wiki/Shamanism_in_Central_Asia "Shamanism in Central Asia"), [Animism](/wiki/Animism "Animism") and [Tengrism](/wiki/Tengrism "Tengrism"). The Turkic animistic traditions were mostly focused on [ancestor worship](/wiki/Ancestor_worship "Ancestor worship"), [polytheistic](/wiki/Polytheistic "Polytheistic")-[animism](/wiki/Animism "Animism") and [shamanism](/wiki/Shamanism "Shamanism"). Later this animistic tradition would form the more organized Tengrism.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] The chief deity was [Tengri](/wiki/Tengri "Tengri"), a [sky god](/wiki/Sky_god "Sky god"), worshipped by the upper classes of early Turkic society until [Manichaeism](/wiki/Manichaeism "Manichaeism") was introduced as the official religion of the [Uyghur Empire](/wiki/Uyghur_Empire "Uyghur Empire") in 763.

The [wolf](/wiki/Wolf "Wolf") symbolizes honour and is also considered the mother of most Turkic peoples. [Ashina](/wiki/Ashina_tribe "Ashina tribe") is the wolf mother of [Tumen Il-Qağan](/wiki/Tumen_Il-Qa%C4%9Fan "Tumen Il-Qağan"), the first Khan of the [Göktürks](/wiki/G%C3%B6kt%C3%BCrks "Göktürks"). The [horse](/wiki/Horse "Horse") and [predatory birds](/wiki/Predatory_birds "Predatory birds"), such as the [eagle](/wiki/Eagle "Eagle") or [falcon](/wiki/Falcon "Falcon"), are also main figures of Turkic mythology.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Religious conversions

#### Buddhism

[Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") played an important role in the history of Turkic peoples, with the first Turkic state adopting and supporting the spread of Buddhism being the Turkic Shahis and the Göktürks. The Göktürks syncretized Buddhism with their traditional religion [Tengrism](/wiki/Tengrism "Tengrism") and also incorporated elements of the Iranian traditional religions, such as [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism"). Buddhism had its height among the [Uyghurs](/wiki/Uyghurs "Uyghurs") in the [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang") region.[[296]](#cite_note-304) Buddhism had also considerable impact and influence onto various other historical Turkic groups. In pre-Islamic times, Buddhism and [Tengrism](/wiki/Tengrism "Tengrism") coexisted, with several Buddhist temples, monasteries, figures and steles, with images of Buddhist characters and sceneries, were constructed by various Turkic tribes. Throughout [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan"), there exist various historical Buddhist sites, including an underground Buddhist cave monastery. After the [Arab conquest of Central Asia](/w/index.php?title=Arab_expansion&action=edit&redlink=1 "Arab expansion (page does not exist)"), and the spread of Islam among locals, Buddhism (and Tengrism) started to lose ground, however a certain influence of the Buddhist teachings remained during the next centuries.[[297]](#cite_note-305)

Tengri Bögü Khan initially made the now extinct [Manichaeism](/wiki/Manichaeism "Manichaeism") the state religion of the [Uyghur Khaganate](/wiki/Uyghur_Khaganate "Uyghur Khaganate") in 763 and it was also popular among the [Karluks](/wiki/Karluks "Karluks"). It was gradually replaced by the [Mahayana Buddhism](/wiki/Mahayana_Buddhism "Mahayana Buddhism").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] It existed in the Buddhist Uyghur [Gaochang](/wiki/Gaochang "Gaochang") up to the 12th century.[[298]](#cite_note-306)

[Tibetan Buddhism](/wiki/Tibetan_Buddhism "Tibetan Buddhism"), or [Vajrayana](/wiki/Vajrayana "Vajrayana") was the main religion after Manichaeism.[[299]](#cite_note-307) They worshipped [Täŋri Täŋrisi Burxan](/wiki/Buddha "Buddha"),[[300]](#cite_note-308) [Quanšï Im Pusar](/wiki/Guanyin "Guanyin")[[301]](#cite_note-309) and [Maitri Burxan](/wiki/Maitreya "Maitreya").[[302]](#cite_note-310) Turkic [Muslim conquest in the Indian subcontinent](/wiki/Muslim_conquest_in_the_Indian_subcontinent "Muslim conquest in the Indian subcontinent") and west [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang") attributed with a rapid and almost total disappearance of it and other religions in North India and Central Asia. The [Sari Uygurs](/wiki/Yugur "Yugur") "Yellow Yughurs" of Western China, as well as the [Tuvans](/wiki/Tuvans "Tuvans") of Russia are the only remaining [Buddhist](/wiki/Buddhist "Buddhist") Turkic peoples.[[303]](#cite_note-311)

#### Islam

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/0/00/Grand_Mosque_in_Astana%2C_Kazakhstan.jpg/250px-Grand_Mosque_in_Astana%2C_Kazakhstan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Grand_Mosque_in_Astana,_Kazakhstan.jpg)

[Astana Grand Mosque](/wiki/Astana_Grand_Mosque "Astana Grand Mosque"), the largest mosque in Central Asia.

Most Turkic people today are [Sunni](/wiki/Sunni "Sunni") [Muslims](/wiki/Muslims "Muslims"), although a significant number in Turkey are [Alevis](/wiki/Alevi "Alevi"). Alevi Turks, who were once primarily dwelling in eastern Anatolia, are today concentrated in major urban centers in western Turkey with the increased urbanism. Turkic Sunni Muslims generally follow the [Hanafi](/wiki/Hanafi "Hanafi") rite. Azeris are traditionally Shiite Muslims. Religious observance is less strict in the Republic of Azerbaijan compared to Iranian Azerbaijan.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Uyghurs_praying.jpg/250px-Uyghurs_praying.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uyghurs_praying.jpg)

Muslim Uyghurs at prayer.

Islam first made contact with the Turkic peoples in 642, when Muslim armies crossed the [Amu Darya](/wiki/Amu_Darya "Amu Darya") after toppling the [Sassanid Empire](/wiki/Sassanid_Empire "Sassanid Empire") the year before. Some of the earliest rulers to convert to Islam were the Turkic princes of the city-states in the region of [Sogdiana](/wiki/Sogdiana "Sogdiana"). Mass conversions did not take place until the [Battle of Talas](/wiki/Battle_of_Talas "Battle of Talas") in 751, in which Turkic tribes sided with the Arabs against Chinese forces, which marked a significant milestone in the history of Islam in the region. From then onwards much of the Turkic heartland became Muslim.[[304]](#cite_note-312) In the 19th century, Turkic Muslim progressives in the Russian Empire spearheaded a reformist movement called [Jadidism](/wiki/Jadidism "Jadidism"), calling for a return to basic Islamic beliefs while simultaneously accepting modernist trends.

#### Christianity

Main article: [Turkic Christians](/wiki/Turkic_Christians "Turkic Christians")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Komrat_-_Katedra_%C5%9Awi%C4%99tego_Jana_Chrzciciela.jpg/250px-Komrat_-_Katedra_%C5%9Awi%C4%99tego_Jana_Chrzciciela.jpg)](/wiki/File:Komrat_-_Katedra_%C5%9Awi%C4%99tego_Jana_Chrzciciela.jpg)

Saint John the Baptist Cathedral in [Gagauzia](/wiki/Gagauzia "Gagauzia")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/75/Gravestone_from_Kirgistan.jpg/250px-Gravestone_from_Kirgistan.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gravestone_from_Kirgistan.jpg)

Gravestone from [Kirgistan](/wiki/Kirgistan "Kirgistan") (thirteenth/fourteenth century) with [Syriac Christian](/wiki/Syriac_Christian "Syriac Christian") inscriptions

The major Christian-Turkic peoples are the [Chuvash](/wiki/Chuvash_people "Chuvash people") of [Chuvashia](/wiki/Chuvashia "Chuvashia") and the [Gagauz](/wiki/Gagauz_people "Gagauz people") (*Gökoğuz*) of [Moldova](/wiki/Moldova "Moldova"), the vast majority of [Chuvash](/wiki/Chuvash_people "Chuvash people") and the [Gagauz](/wiki/Gagauz_people "Gagauz people") are [Eastern Orthodox Christians](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Church "Eastern Orthodox Church").[[305]](#cite_note-313)[[306]](#cite_note-Cole_2011_74-314)[[307]](#cite_note-315) The traditional religion of the [Chuvash](/wiki/Chuvash_people "Chuvash people") of Russia, while containing many ancient Turkic concepts, also shares some elements with [Zoroastrianism](/wiki/Zoroastrianism "Zoroastrianism"), [Khazar](/wiki/Khazar "Khazar") Judaism, and Islam.
The Chuvash converted to [Eastern Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox_Christianity "Eastern Orthodox Christianity") for the most part in the second half of the 19th century.[[306]](#cite_note-Cole_2011_74-314) As a result, festivals and rites were made to coincide with Orthodox feasts, and Christian rites replaced their traditional counterparts. A minority of the Chuvash still profess their traditional faith.[[308]](#cite_note-316) Between the 9th and 14th centuries, [Church of the East](/wiki/Church_of_the_East "Church of the East") was popular among Turks such as the [Naimans](/wiki/Naimans "Naimans").[[309]](#cite_note-317) It even revived in Gaochang and expanded in [Xinjiang](/wiki/Xinjiang "Xinjiang") in the [Yuan dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") period.[[310]](#cite_note-318)[[311]](#cite_note-319)[[312]](#cite_note-320) It disappeared after its collapse.[[313]](#cite_note-321)[[314]](#cite_note-322)

[Kryashens](/wiki/Kryashens "Kryashens") are a sub-group of the [Volga Tatars](/wiki/Volga_Tatars "Volga Tatars"), and the vast majority are [Orthodox Christians](/wiki/Russian_Orthodoxy "Russian Orthodoxy").[[315]](#cite_note-323) [Nağaybäk](/wiki/Na%C4%9Fayb%C3%A4k "Nağaybäk") are an indigenous Turkic people in Russia, most Nağaybäk are Christian and were largely converted during the 18th century.[[316]](#cite_note-324) Many [Volga Tatars](/wiki/Volga_Tatars "Volga Tatars") were [Christianized](/wiki/Christianized "Christianized") by [Ivan the Terrible](/wiki/Ivan_the_Terrible "Ivan the Terrible") during the 16th century, and continued to Christianized under subsequent Russian rulers and Orthodox clergy up to the mid-eighteenth century.[[317]](#cite_note-325)

#### Animism

Today there are several groups that support a revival of the ancient traditions. Especially after the collapse of the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), many in [Central Asia](/wiki/Central_Asia "Central Asia") converted or openly practice animistic and shamanistic rituals. It is estimated that about 60% of [Kyrgyz people](/wiki/Kyrgyz_people "Kyrgyz people") practice a form of animistic rituals. In [Kazakhstan](/wiki/Kazakhstan "Kazakhstan") there are about 54,000 followers of the ancient traditions.[[318]](#cite_note-326)[[319]](#cite_note-327)

#### Muslim Turks and non-Muslim Turks

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/42/Uighur_Prince.jpg/250px-Uighur_Prince.jpg)](/wiki/File:Uighur_Prince.jpg)

An [Old Uyghur](/wiki/Uyghur_Khaganate "Uyghur Khaganate") [Khagan](/wiki/Khagan "Khagan")

The Uyghur Turks, who once belonged to a variety of religions, were gradually Islamized during a period spanning the 10th and 13th centuries. Some scholars have linked the phenomenon of recently Islamized Uyghur soldiers recruited by the [Mongol Empire](/wiki/Mongol_Empire "Mongol Empire") to the slow conversion of Uyghur populations to Islam.[[320]](#cite_note-328)[[321]](#cite_note-329)

The non-Muslim Turks' worship of [Tengri](/wiki/Tengri "Tengri") and other gods was mocked and insulted by the Muslim Turk [Mahmud al-Kashgari](/wiki/Mahmud_al-Kashgari "Mahmud al-Kashgari"), who wrote a verse referring to them – *The Infidels – May God destroy them!*[[322]](#cite_note-330)[[323]](#cite_note-Dankoff_68–80-331)

The Basmil, Yabāḳu and Uyghur states were among the Turkic peoples who fought against the Kara-Khanids spread of Islam. The Islamic Kara-Khanids were made out of [Tukhsi](/wiki/Tukhsi "Tukhsi"), Yaghma, Çiğil and Karluk.[[324]](#cite_note-GibbLewis1998-332)

Kashgari claimed that the Prophet assisted in a miraculous event where 700,000 Yabāqu infidels were defeated by 40,000 Muslims led by Arslān Tegīn claiming that fires shot sparks from gates located on a green mountain towards the Yabāqu.[[325]](#cite_note-333) The Yabaqu were a Turkic people.[[326]](#cite_note-334)

[Mahmud al-Kashgari](/wiki/Mahmud_al-Kashgari "Mahmud al-Kashgari") insulted the Uyghur Buddhists as "Uighur dogs" and called them "Tats", which referred to the "Uighur infidels" according to the Tuxsi and Taghma, while other Turks called Persians "tat".[[327]](#cite_note-335)[[328]](#cite_note-336) While Kashgari displayed a different attitude towards the Turks diviners beliefs and "national customs", he expressed towards Buddhism a hatred in his Diwan where he wrote the verse cycle on the war against Uighur Buddhists. Buddhist origin words like toyin (a cleric or priest) and Burxān or Furxan (meaning Buddha, acquiring the generic meaning of "idol" in the Turkic language of Kashgari) had negative connotations to Muslim Turks.[[329]](#cite_note-337)[[323]](#cite_note-Dankoff_68–80-331)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6e/Tyurki.jpg/250px-Tyurki.jpg)](/wiki/File:Tyurki.jpg)

Göktürk petroglyphs from Mongolia (6th to 8th century)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Panjakent_man_dressed_in_%E2%80%9CTurkic%E2%80%9C_long_coats%2C_6th-8th_c.jpg/250px-Panjakent_man_dressed_in_%E2%80%9CTurkic%E2%80%9C_long_coats%2C_6th-8th_c.jpg)](/wiki/File:Panjakent_man_dressed_in_%E2%80%9CTurkic%E2%80%9C_long_coats,_6th-8th_c.jpg)
